38	
39	# Silicon Photonics Supply Chain: 2026–2030 Investment Outlook
40	
41	## Executive Summary  
42	Silicon photonics is on the cusp of a significant growth surge as data centers and AI infrastructure shift from copper to optical interconnects. The driving force is the so-called “copper wall” – physical limits of electrical links (resistance, heat, bandwidth) that waste energy and bottleneck modern AI clusters. Optical links, using light instead of electrons, can carry far more data with lower latency and power, making them essential for scaling next-generation networks. By 2026, over half of all optical transceiver sales are expected to use silicon photonics, up from just one-third in 2024. Industry forecasts project the global silicon photonics market to continue its ~20–25% CAGR, reaching the multi-billion dollar range by the late 2020s. This boom is underpinned by the exponential bandwidth demand of AI and cloud applications, with 200G per lane optical channels becoming mainstream by 2026–27 and paving the way for 800G and 1.6T transceivers. Co-packaged optics (CPO) – integrating photonics directly with switch/processor ASICs – is targeted for large-scale adoption around 2028–2030 to overcome the performance and energy limits of pluggable modules. 
43	
44	Every layer of the silicon photonics supply chain stands to benefit. **Photonic chip design** houses are delivering the intellectual property and specialized circuits that drive these optical engines. **Fabrication (foundries)** are rapidly expanding capacity on mature nodes optimized for photonic integrated circuits (PICs). **Packaging & assembly** specialists (OSATs) are reinventing how chips and lasers are aligned and integrated at scale. **Testing** equipment providers are addressing new challenges in optical alignment and high-volume photonics test to ensure yield and reliability. **Transceiver module developers** – both established optical component firms and startups – are racing to supply 800G/1600G modules for cloud providers and to prototype CPO engines. Finally, **systems integrators** (network equipment and AI system vendors) are forging partnerships across the supply chain to embed silicon photonics in switches, servers, and HPC clusters, seeking competitive advantage in performance per watt. Table 1 summarizes key players in each segment driving this trend.
45	
46	**Table 1 – Key Companies Across the Silicon Photonics Supply Chain (2026–2030)**
47	
48	| Supply Chain Segment       | Notable Companies (Public/Private)                                       | Strategic Position (Investment Relevance)                                 |
49	|----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|
50	| **Photonic IC Design**     | *Ayar Labs* (Private), *Lightmatter* (Private), *Celestial AI* (Private), *Intel* (NASDAQ: INTC) | Silicon photonics **design leaders** developing optical I/O and AI chips; startups backed by major tech firms (e.g. NVIDIA, Intel) with unique IP (optical chiplets, photonic computing) poised for **high growth**. Intel’s internal silicon photonics R&D provides a **strategic edge** in optical data center connectivity. |
51	| **Fabrication (Foundries)**| *GlobalFoundries* (NASDAQ: GFS), *TSMC* (TWSE: 2330/NYSE: TSM), *Tower Semiconductor* (NASDAQ: TSEM), *STMicroelectronics* (NYSE: STM), *Intel* (NASDAQ: INTC), *SilTerra* (Private) | Major semiconductor **foundries scaling SiPh wafer manufacturing**. GF’s recent acquisition of Singapore’s AMF makes it the world’s largest pure-play silicon photonics foundry, targeting $1B+ in photonics revenue by 2030. TSMC leads in co-packaged optics development (partnering with NVIDIA/Broadcom). Tower Semi is tripling photonics capacity by mid-2026 and offers unique SiGe-integrated processes for ultrafast optical modulators. STMicro re-entered silicon photonics in 2025, and regional players like SilTerra (Malaysia) have expanded 200mm photonic fabs. These foundries are **well-positioned for high-volume PIC production** as demand soars. |
52	| **Packaging & Assembly**   | *ASE Technology* (NYSE: ASX), *Fabrinet* (NYSE: FN), *POET Technologies* (NASDAQ: POET), *Jabil* (NYSE: JBL) | Specialized **OSATs and contract manufacturers** enabling high-precision optical packaging. ASE has demonstrated advanced co-packaged optics techniques to improve AI data center energy efficiency. Fabrinet, a top optical assembly house, is relied on by NVIDIA to assemble complex optical interconnects with micron-level laser alignment and is gearing up as a primary assembly partner for the first CPO deployments. POET Technologies offers an “Optical Interposer” platform allowing passive flip-chip bonding of lasers to photonic chips, which recently secured volume orders for 1.6T optical engines needed in next-gen AI clusters. These companies address the **scaling bottleneck** of photonics packaging, a critical chokepoint for volume adoption. |
53	| **Testing & Equipment**    | *Teradyne* (NASDAQ: TER), *Aehr Test Systems* (NASDAQ: AEHR), *Keysight* (NYSE: KEYS), *ficonTEC* (Private) | **Test automation leaders** tackling the unique challenges of silicon photonics. Teradyne notes that current photonics test is highly manual and must evolve for high-volume CPO manufacturing – automated systems need to handle optical alignments, thermal control, and mixed signal (electrical/optical) testing. Aehr Test’s wafer-level burn-in systems (FOX platform) are being adapted for optical ICs, aiming to ensure known-good photonic dies at scale. Keysight and others provide high-speed optical analyzers and network test gear to validate 800G+ links. ficonTEC (private) supplies specialized assembly and test equipment for photonics, enabling precision optical alignment in production. **Investments in test capability** are crucial to safeguard yield as photonic component output ramps up. |
54	| **Transceiver Modules**    | *Coherent Corp.* (NASDAQ: COHR), *Lumentum* (NASDAQ: LITE), *InnoLight (TeraHop)* (Private), *Cisco* (NASDAQ: CSCO), *Marvell* (NASDAQ: MRVL)* | **Optical transceiver makers** delivering the pluggable modules and optical engines for data centers. Coherent (formerly II-VI + Finisar) is a vertically integrated volume leader (~25% share) that manufactures lasers, wafers, and finished modules – positioned to supply hyperscalers at scale and racing to lead in 1.6T pluggables. Lumentum (after Oclaro/NeoPhotonics acquisitions) is a top laser and modulators provider, dominating high-speed EML laser chips essential for 800G+ modules. China’s InnoLight (rebranded *TeraHop*) along with Hisense and Accelink ship millions of modules for AI interconnects, bolstered by domestic initiatives. Cisco has acquired silicon photonics firms (Luxtera, Acacia) and develops its own modules for switches, leveraging internal design plus contract manufacturing. Marvell, via its Inphi acquisition, supplies advanced DSPs and analog ICs for 800G optical links and is collaborating on CPO solutions (partnering with Broadcom or others). The **transceiver segment** stands to grow rapidly as cloud operators upgrade an installed base of 400G to 800G/1600G optics, and as co-packaged optical engines gradually enter the market. |
55	| **Systems Integration**    | *Cisco* (NASDAQ: CSCO), *Arista Networks* (NYSE: ANET), *Juniper Networks* (NYSE: JNPR), *NVIDIA* (NASDAQ: NVDA), *Ciena* (NYSE: CIEN), *Broadcom* (NASDAQ: AVGO)* | **Network and HPC system leaders** steering silicon photonics adoption in end-use systems. Cisco, Arista, and Juniper dominate data center switching and are integrating silicon photonic interfaces to achieve higher bandwidth density in future hardware (e.g. exploring on-board optics and CPO for switch ASICs). NVIDIA – facing massive GPU clustering needs – has invested in optical interconnect startups (e.g. Ayar Labs) and relies on photonics specialists (like Fabrinet and Coherent) to build the links for AI supercomputers. Broadcom, as the leading switch chip supplier, is effectively **forcing the market** toward co-packaged optics by pairing its switch silicon with optical engines – its partnerships with TSMC and others on CPO are well underway. Ciena, a telecom optical systems giant, is expanding inside the data center by acquiring photonics startups (e.g. its 2025 purchase of Nubis Communications) to offer **high-performance AI interconnect solutions**. In sum, system integrators are aligning with photonics suppliers through partnerships and acquisitions to ensure they can deliver the low-latency, high-bandwidth networks that next-generation AI and cloud services will require. |
56	
57	*(*Note: Marvell and Broadcom are primarily chip and component suppliers, but they are included here for their strategic roles in enabling system-level photonics integration.)*
58	
59	---
60	
61	## Photonic Chip Design & IP Innovation  
62	Silicon photonics design is a critical upstream segment, providing the **intellectual property and specialized chip designs** that fuel the entire supply chain. This layer includes companies designing photonic integrated circuits (PICs) – such as optical modulators, switches, and light-based computing elements – often in fabless models. With Moore’s Law slowing, the industry is investing heavily in novel photonic architectures to break through the bandwidth and energy limits of electrical chips. From optical I/O chiplets that replace traditional copper interconnects, to photonic computing processors that use light for AI computations, these innovators are poised for rapid growth as silicon photonics moves from niche to mainstream.
63	
64	**Leading Companies:**  
65	- **Ayar Labs (Private)** – A Silicon Valley startup focusing on optical I/O chiplets (TeraPHY) that enable chips to communicate using light instead of copper. Ayar’s technology eliminates electrical I/O bottlenecks by integrating optical links directly onto packages. The company has strong strategic backing – its recent $155 million Series D was led by semiconductor incumbents (AMD, Intel, NVIDIA) and others, valuing Ayar above $1 billion. This reflects industry confidence that optical chiplet interconnects will be *essential* for large-scale AI and HPC systems. Ayar has partnered with Intel and Hewlett Packard Enterprise on demonstrations of optical-enabled processors, and its chips are fabricated by GlobalFoundries in the U.S., highlighting an advantage in supply chain integration. As generative AI drives “AI-native” datacenter designs, Ayar Labs is well positioned with a unique technology to relieve networking bottlenecks. Investors view Ayar as a pure-play bet on the silicon photonics adoption curve in data centers.  
66	
67	- **Lightmatter (Private)** – A leading photonic computing startup building photonic processors and interconnects for AI datacenters. Lightmatter’s photonic chips use light to perform matrix multiplication and to move data between chips at ultra-high bandwidth, dramatically improving performance-per-watt for AI workloads. The company’s promise and traction are evidenced by a massive **$400 million Series D raise in late 2024 at a $4.4 billion valuation**, backed by investors like T. Rowe Price, Fidelity, and GV (Alphabet). This brought total funding to ~$850 million, aimed at scaling manufacturing and deployment of its photonic chips in customer data centers. Lightmatter’s first large clusters using its photonic interconnected systems are expected online by 2025. With a former NVIDIA networking executive as its CFO and an IPO envisioned, Lightmatter is seen as an emerging **market leader in photonic AI accelerators and networking**. Its strategy is to be vendor-agnostic – providing optical interconnect tech that can plug into any vendor’s AI chips (NVIDIA, Intel, AMD, etc.). If successful, Lightmatter could become a core supplier of photonic engines to hyperscalers racing to deploy bigger and more efficient AI infrastructure.
68	
69	- **Celestial AI (Private)** – A fast-rising startup behind the *Photonic Fabric™* optical interconnect platform. Celestial’s technology targets the “memory wall” and “network wall” in AI computing by using light to move data between processors and memory with extremely high throughput. The company has attracted *very large funding* – over **$250 million in Series C** rounds (C and C1) in 2023–24, including investments from BlackRock, Fidelity, Koch Industries, and strategic backers like AMD. Its post-money valuation reportedly reached $2.5 billion, reflecting high expectations. Celestial AI’s solution can be integrated into AI servers to optically connect GPUs/TPUs and memory, reducing communication latency and boosting utilization of expensive AI chips. With this capital, the company is accelerating product development and go-to-market to address hyperscale data center needs. Celestial AI is noteworthy for investors due to its deep bench of backers and its focus on interconnects – a critical enabler as AI models scale and require distributed compute across optical fabrics.
70	
71	- **OpenLight (Private)** – A unique player offering an *open silicon photonics platform* with integrated lasers. Spun out of Synopsys in 2022, OpenLight provides a process design kit (PDK) and manufacturing capability for photonic ASICs (“PASICs”), allowing customers to design custom silicon photonic chips with **heterogeneously integrated indium phosphide lasers and amplifiers on silicon**. In August 2025 OpenLight raised $34 million in Series A funding from investors including HPE (via its Juniper Networks unit) and others. Its model is part design-house and part foundry-enabler: OpenLight works with Tower Semiconductor as the fab partner and has validated its laser-integrated PDK on Tower’s process. This gives smaller companies a fast track to build photonic chips with lasers, modulators, detectors, etc., without building their own fabs. OpenLight’s early customers span networking equipment firms and even hyperscalers, and production chips are expected by end of 2025. For the supply chain, OpenLight fills an important niche by **democratizing photonics design**, and for investors it represents an enabling pick-and-shovel play as more companies seek to develop custom optical engines.
72	
73	- **Intel (Public: INTC)** – As a pioneering giant in silicon photonics, Intel continues to be highly relevant at the design stage. Intel was one of the first to commercialize silicon photonics (launching 100G pluggable modules years ago) and has developed a rich IP portfolio (high-speed modulators, germanium photodetectors, etc.). Despite corporate restructuring, Intel maintains its internal silicon photonics fabs and R&D, focusing on both data center optics and new areas like co-packaged optics for its switches and future server CPUs. Intel’s approach is vertically integrated – designing and **manufacturing its own photonic transceiver chips**, which it uses in products and sometimes sells on the merchant market. For example, Intel’s 800G pluggable modules and its future on-package optical I/O solutions stem from this in-house design capability. Intel’s market positioning is both as a supplier and a consumer of silicon photonics: any investor evaluating the ecosystem should note that Intel’s commitment signals confidence in the technology’s necessity for scaling compute clusters. While not a pure-play photonics stock, Intel’s moves (and partnerships – e.g. with Ayar Labs for optical chiplets) often validate the broader trend and can create opportunities for its partners and suppliers.
74	
75	*Other notable design-stage players include **IBM Research**, which has long innovated in silicon photonic circuitry (e.g. optical interconnects for mainframes and quantum computing networks), and startups like **Luminous Computing** and **Lightelligence**, also developing photonic AI chips. These firms, along with university spin-offs, contribute to a healthy pipeline of photonic design innovation, though the ones highlighted above are among those best positioned for 2026–2030 growth.* 
76	
77	## Silicon Photonics Fabrication (Foundries)  
78	High-volume manufacturing of photonic chips is a cornerstone of the supply chain. Unlike traditional logic chips, silicon photonics leverages mature CMOS nodes (often 45nm to 90nm processes on silicon-on-insulator) with added process steps for optical structures. This means **specialized foundry capacity** is needed – and leading semiconductor foundries worldwide are now racing to add that capacity, eyeing the coming wave of optical transceiver and CPO demand. The competitiveness of each foundry lies in its process IP (low-loss waveguides, efficient modulators, laser integration techniques) and its ability to scale production with high yield. Investors are focusing on which foundries will capture the surge in volume for optics, similar to how TSMC captured mobile chip growth in the last decade.
79	
80	**Leading Companies:**  
81	- **GlobalFoundries (NYSE: GFS)** – GF has made one of the **boldest commitments to silicon photonics**, aiming to corner this market. In November 2025, GlobalFoundries announced the acquisition of Advanced Micro Foundry (AMF) in Singapore, a pure-play photonics foundry, propelling GF to the #1 position globally for silicon photonics manufacturing. AMF had built a successful business with 300+ photonics customers and advanced capabilities (including integrating indium phosphide lasers onto silicon). With AMF, GF gains a proven 200 mm production line (to be upgraded to 300 mm) and a wealth of photonics IP, which GF will leverage alongside its existing 300 mm photonics line in New York. GF’s “Fotonix” platform – a monolithic photonics+RF CMOS process – is already an industry standard for fabless firms that lack their own fabs. The company expects **over $1 billion in silicon photonics revenue by 2030**, and is marketing itself as a U.S.-friendly, end-to-end source for optical chip production. With geopolitical factors making supply chain security important, GF’s expansion (offering a “China-free” photonics supply chain) could attract North American and European customers. For investors, GF’s photonics push represents a growth engine on top of its existing specialty semiconductor business – they are effectively positioning to be the TSMC of photonics in high-volume “feature-rich” nodes.
82	
83	- **TSMC (TWSE: 2330 / NYSE: TSM)** – The world’s largest semiconductor foundry is also actively involved in silicon photonics, albeit more quietly. TSMC’s core business is leading-edge logic, but it has been investing in photonics as a complementary offering. Notably, TSMC has taken a lead in **co-packaged optics (CPO) development**, working with networking leaders like NVIDIA and Broadcom on optical integration for next-gen switches. TSMC views CPO as “another packaging technology” to offer clients, aligning with its advanced packaging services (e.g. TSMC’s InFO and CoWoS are analogous in chiplet integration). In practice, TSMC can provide front-end fabrication of silicon photonic engines as well as back-end integration of those engines with ASICs. For example, TSMC’s involvement in NVIDIA’s 2025 CPO demonstrations suggests it fabricated the optical engines or at least handled the packaging substrate. TSMC is also rumored to offer photonic process nodes (e.g. a 300 mm silicon photonics platform) to high-volume customers, though details are not public. Given TSMC’s manufacturing prowess, if demand for optical I/O on chips explodes, TSMC will capture a significant share – especially from customers who already use TSMC for logic and want tight integration. From an investment perspective, TSMC’s photonics foray reinforces its role as a one-stop-shop for high-performance chip needs, though photonics remains a small part of its revenue in the near term.
84	
85	- **Tower Semiconductor (NASDAQ: TSEM)** – A specialty foundry (based in Israel, recently courted by Intel for acquisition) that has *quietly become a significant silicon photonics fab*. Tower operates multiple 200 mm fabs (in California, Texas, Israel) and a 300 mm fab in Japan, all supporting silicon photonics processes. The company is expanding aggressively: Tower plans to **double SiPho capacity by end of 2025 and triple it by mid-2026** as part of a $300 million investment. It even expects to double its photonics revenue in 2026, challenging GF for the top spot. Tower’s differentiator is its **open-process model** and materials expertise. It allows customers to integrate high-speed SiGe (silicon germanium) BiCMOS electronics with silicon photonic waveguides on the same chip. This is crucial because at 1.6 Tb/s and beyond, pure silicon modulators struggle with speed, whereas SiGe can drive modulators at 100+ GHz. Tower recently announced a breakthrough in *wafer-scale laser bonding*, enabling lasers to be attached to photonic wafers in mass (reducing the cost of integrating lasers for 1.6T modules). With these innovations, Tower is seen as an **“artisan” foundry** offering flexible, high-performance photonics processes for customers who need more customization than GF or TSMC provide. For investors, Tower’s photonics business could be a strong growth story (even as its core CMOS business is steady) – it’s effectively carving out a high-value niche in the broader foundry market. News of any major Tier-1 customer wins (as hinted for its new CPO process) could be catalysts for Tower’s stock.
86	
87	- **STMicroelectronics (NYSE: STM)** – Europe’s large semiconductor firm re-entered the silicon photonics market in early 2025 after having exited some years prior. STMicro has a history in photonics (it previously partnered on photonic projects) and now sees renewed opportunity as optical interconnects go mainstream. The company is reportedly leveraging its 300 mm fab in Crolles, France, to offer silicon photonics manufacturing, possibly targeting applications like optical networking and even sensors (ST is strong in automotive/industrial, where LiDAR and sensors using Si photonics could emerge). In 2025, STMicro announced partnerships to commercialize photonic technology (one example: teaming with Ayar Labs to evaluate optical I/O for European customers). While details are sparse, ST’s re-entry indicates **no major region wants to be left out** of photonics production – and the company could become a key supplier in the EU, supported by initiatives to build a European photonics supply chain. Investment-wise, STMicro’s photonics activity is one facet of its diversification (the company is also in power electronics, microcontrollers, etc.), but any significant contracts or progress in this area could add upside to its growth projections for late-decade.
88	
89	- **Intel Foundry Services (IFS)** – In addition to Intel’s in-house use mentioned earlier, Intel’s fabs (especially its D1 facility in Oregon) have dedicated lines for silicon photonics. Intel historically built all its own transceiver chips, making it somewhat vertically integrated. As Intel opens up foundry services, it’s conceivable it may offer photonic manufacturing to select customers (particularly those in the U.S. requiring a domestic source). In any case, Intel’s onshore photonics fab capacity is a strategic asset. The Reuters report on Ayar Labs highlights that *GlobalFoundries’ New York fab and Intel are both strategic investors*, and Ayar’s chips are made at GF. This implies Intel’s photonics fab is currently more for internal use, with GF picking up external fabless business like Ayar. Going forward, if Intel’s own photonic product needs fluctuate, IFS could potentially take on foundry contracts in this domain (especially given government interest in U.S.-based photonics for defense and HPC). For now, Intel’s photonic fab presence mainly underscores the **demand for capacity** – multiple players are needed to meet industry volume requirements in coming years.
90	
91	*In addition to these, regional foundries such as **SilTerra** (Malaysia, 200 mm photonics line, now part of DNeX/Foxconn), **CompoundTek** (Singapore, a smaller pure-play photonics foundry), and **SMIC** in China (which is reportedly developing indigenous photonic processes) are part of the global supply base. As Yole’s analysts note, China is scaling up domestic capabilities aggressively – with companies like HiSilicon, Shawcor (Hefei), and others building out photonic fabs – aiming to reduce reliance on Western foundries. This global competition will likely intensify, but from an investment perspective, the *leading foundry players (GF, TSMC, Tower, etc.) with proven technology and capacity are expected to capture the bulk of the high-value photonics manufacturing growth from 2026–2030.**
92	
93	## Advanced Packaging & Assembly  
94	Packaging is often considered the **Achilles’ heel** of silicon photonics scaling. Attaching delicate optical fibers, aligning lasers to waveguides, and integrating photonic chips with electronic drivers require precision assembly techniques far beyond conventional semiconductor packaging. Historically, optical packaging was a manual, labor-intensive process (often “paying a PhD to glue fibers under a microscope” as one analyst quipped). To meet data center volumes, the industry needs automated, high-throughput packaging solutions – and this challenge is giving rise to both **innovations in packaging technology and the rise of specialist contractors**. The strategic importance of this layer cannot be overstated: if packaging yields or throughput falter, it becomes a bottleneck that slows product shipments. Investors are therefore keen on companies that can solve photonic packaging at scale, as they are linchpins for the entire supply chain’s growth.
95	
96	**Leading Companies:**  
97	- **ASE Technology (NYSE: ASX)** – The world’s largest semiconductor assembly and test provider (OSAT), ASE has been at the forefront of adapting mainstream packaging for silicon photonics. ASE has developed processes for co-packaged optics (CPO) and optical *System-in-Package* solutions, leveraging its expertise in 2.5D/3D integration. In 2023, ASE demonstrated a CPO platform that integrates optical engines closely around an AI chip, significantly improving energy efficiency and bandwidth density for AI systems. The company highlights that shortening electrical paths via on-package optics can dramatically improve pJ/bit and $/Gbps metrics compared to pluggable modules. Key building blocks provided by ASE include high-accuracy flip-chip bonding for lasers and photonic dies, wafer-level optical testing (to ensure known-good die before assembly), and advanced substrates that accommodate optical fiber attach points. ASE’s involvement gives silicon photonics customers a high-volume assembly partner who can implement novel designs in a manufacturing-friendly way. As the industry moves toward CPO around 2028, ASE stands to gain from packaging those optical-chiplet-on-ASIC solutions. From an investment perspective, ASE’s diversification into photonics packaging adds to its growth drive
98	
99	---

toolCalls.result:
1	---
2	date: 2025-11-26
3	tags: [research, semiconductors, photonics, CPO, silicon-photonics, gemini-canvas]
4	status: active
5	sector: Semiconductors & Photonics
6	source: Gemini Canvas export
7	---
8	
9	# Silicon Photonics Supply Chain: The Investment Architecture for the Post-Copper Era
10	
11	## 1. Executive Strategic Overview: The Inevitability of Light
12	
13	The global digital infrastructure is currently colliding with a fundamental limit of physics known in the semiconductor industry as the "Copper Wall." For decades, the movement of data between processors, memory, and switches has relied on the flow of electrons through copper traces and cables. As the industry advances into the era of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and hyperscale computing, demanding data transfer rates of 800 Gigabits per second (Gbps), 1.6 Terabits (Tbps), and beyond, copper interconnects are becoming physically untenable. They are too slow, too hot, and too limited in reach to support the "Million-GPU" clusters required to train next-generation Large Language Models (LLMs).
14	
15	This physical bottleneck has catalyzed a structural shift toward Silicon Photonics (SiPh)—a technology that manufactures optical devices using standard silicon semiconductor processes. This transition is not merely a component upgrade; it is a wholesale architectural reimagining of the data center, moving the optical interface from the rack faceplate directly into the processor package. This report provides an exhaustive investment analysis of the silicon photonics supply chain, identifying the constituents positioned to capture value in this multi-year secular trend.
16	
17	The investment thesis is grounded in three critical pillars:
18	1.  **The AI Scaling Imperative:** AI model complexity is growing 32-fold every three years.[1] To support this, interconnect bandwidth must scale exponentially. Optical interconnects are the only viable path to achieving the required bandwidth density and energy efficiency (picojoules per bit) for future AI clusters.
19	2.  **The Rise of Co-Packaged Optics (CPO):** The industry is migrating from pluggable transceivers to Co-Packaged Optics, where the optical engine is integrated with the host ASIC. This shift redistributes the profit pool from traditional module makers to semiconductor foundries, advanced packaging providers, and switch silicon vendors.[2, 3]
20	3.  **Sovereign Supply Chain Reconstruction:** Geopolitical friction is driving a bifurcation of the supply chain, creating distinct investment opportunities in Western-aligned manufacturing (e.g., GlobalFoundries, Fabrinet, Amkor) versus the dominant incumbent Chinese module ecosystem (InnoLight, Eoptolink).[4, 5]
21	
22	Current market forecasts project the silicon photonics market to grow from approximately $3.11 billion in 2025 to over $10 billion by 2030, with some aggressive estimates reaching nearly $29 billion by 2034.[4, 6, 7] This growth trajectory signifies an inflection point where photonics moves from a niche technology to a ubiquitous utility in the compute fabric.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## 2. The Physics and Economics of the Interconnect Transition
27	
28	To accurately assess the investment potential of specific companies, one must first understand the technical friction driving the market. The incumbent technology—copper—is failing due to signal attenuation and power consumption.
29	
30	### 2.1 The Copper Wall and Signal Integrity
31	In traditional data center architectures, electrical signals travel from a switch ASIC, through a PCB, to a connector, and then across a copper cable. At lower speeds (10G, 25G), copper is highly conductive and efficient. However, at the high frequencies required for 112G and 224G SerDes (Serializer/Deserializer) lanes, the "skin effect" and dielectric loss cause the signal to degrade rapidly.
32	
33	At 224Gbps, a copper signal can barely travel a few inches on a standard PCB before becoming indistinguishable from noise. To counteract this, chip designers must employ increasingly complex Digital Signal Processors (DSPs) to re-time and equalize the signal. These DSPs are power-hungry. In a traditional transceiver-based switch, the power consumed just to move data to the edge of the box is approaching 30-40% of the total system power.[8] This is energy not used for compute, but wasted on transport.
34	
35	### 2.2 The Energy Equation: Picojoules per Bit (pJ/bit)
36	The core metric for AI infrastructure is energy efficiency, measured in picojoules per bit (pJ/bit).
37	*   **Electrical Interconnects:** Typically consume 10-20 pJ/bit for board-level reach.
38	*   **Pluggable Optics:** Reduce this for longer reaches but still require power-intensive DSPs at the edge.
39	*   **Silicon Photonics (CPO/Optical I/O):** Targets <5 pJ/bit by eliminating the long electrical trace and the need for heavy signal conditioning.[9]
40	
41	For an AI cluster with thousands of XPUs (accelerators), this efficiency delta translates to megawatts of power. Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang noted that if the NVL72 rack system had used traditional optics instead of copper for its specific short-reach backplane, it would have required an additional 20 kilowatts of power—illustrating the complex trade-offs currently being managed. However, as scaling goes beyond the rack, optics become mandatory. Ayar Labs and other disruptors contend that integrated optical I/O is the only way to break the power-bandwidth bottleneck permanently.[1]
42	
43	### 2.3 The Latency Imperative
44	AI training is a synchronous workload. Thousands of GPUs must update their weights simultaneously. If one link is slow, the entire cluster waits. Traditional transceivers introduce latency via the DSP and the physical distance. Silicon photonics, specifically architectures that remove the DSP (like Linear Drive Optics) or integrate it (CPO), drastically reduce latency. Integrated silicon photonics can achieve latencies of less than 100ns, a critical threshold for maintaining high utilization in AI clusters.[9]
45	
46	---
47	
48	## 3. Market Dynamics and Quantitative Forecasts
49	
50	The silicon photonics market is entering a phase of hyper-growth, but the landscape is nuanced by varying adoption rates of different technologies.
51	
52	### 3.1 Market Sizing and CAGR
53	Analyst consensus points to a market growing at roughly 25-30% annually.
54	*   **MarketsandMarkets** projects a jump from $2.65 billion in 2025 to $9.65 billion by 2030 (CAGR ~30%).[6]
55	*   **Precedence Research** is more bullish, forecasting $28.75 billion by 2034, driven by the ubiquity of optical I/O in consumer devices beyond the data center.[7]
56	*   **Mordor Intelligence** estimates the 2025 market at $3.11 billion, growing to $10.36 billion by 2030.[4]
57	
58	These figures suggest that while the base is currently modest compared to the broader semiconductor industry, the velocity of capital allocation is accelerating.
59	
60	### 3.2 Segmentation by Data Rate
61	The investment cycle is defined by transceiver speeds:
62	*   **400G:** The current volume workhorse. Commodity status is approaching, leading to margin compression for suppliers.
63	*   **800G:** The current high-growth segment. Ramping heavily in 2024-2025, driven by Nvidia's H100/H200 deployments. Suppliers like InnoLight and Eoptolink dominate here.[10]
64	*   **1.6T:** The next frontier. Expected to see initial volumes in late 2025/2026. This generation acts as the bridge to CPO.
65	*   **3.2T and Beyond:** The CPO era. Traditional pluggables become physically too large and power-inefficient for the faceplate density required.
66	
67	### 3.3 Geographic Drivers
68	*   **North America:** Leads in demand (Hyperscalers: Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta) and design innovation (Nvidia, Broadcom, Marvell). Accounts for ~38% of the market.[4]
69	*   **Asia Pacific:** Leads in manufacturing and module assembly. China is aggressively investing in domestic silicon photonics to reduce reliance on Western DSPs and lasers.[11]
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 4. Technological Architectures: The Battle for the Socket
74	
75	The silicon photonics market is not a monolith; it is a battleground between competing architectures. Understanding these is crucial for stock selection.
76	
77	### 4.1 Pluggable Optics (The Incumbent)
78	This is the current standard. A module plugs into the switch faceplate.
79	*   **Pros:** Flexibility, serviceability (if a module breaks, you replace it), established ecosystem.
80	*   **Cons:** Power inefficient, faceplate density limits.
81	*   **Investment Implications:** Beneficiaries are **InnoLight**, **Coherent**, **Lumentum**, **Fabrinet**. This segment remains robust through the 800G and early 1.6T cycle but faces long-term existential risk from CPO.
82	
83	### 4.2 Linear Pluggable Optics (LPO)
84	A halfway house between standard pluggables and CPO. LPO removes the DSP from the optical module, relying on the switch ASIC's SerDes to drive the optics directly.
85	*   **Pros:** Lower power, lower latency, lower cost (no DSP in module).
86	*   **Cons:** Requires very high-quality electrical channels; interoperability challenges.
87	*   **Investment Implications:** **Macom** and **Credo** are key players here, alongside module makers adapting to LPO designs. It extends the life of the pluggable form factor.[12]
88	
89	### 4.3 Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)
90	The optical engine moves inside the package, sitting next to the switch ASIC.
91	*   **Pros:** Maximum energy efficiency, highest bandwidth density.
92	*   **Cons:** Thermal management (lasers hate heat), serviceability (if optics fail, the whole switch might need replacement), ecosystem immaturity.
93	*   **Investment Implications:** **Broadcom** and **Marvell** are the drivers. **TSMC** and **Advanced Packaging** firms (Amkor, Besi) are the enablers. This is the highest-value long-term bet.[2, 13]
94	
95	### 4.4 Optical I/O and Wafer-Scale Integration
96	The most disruptive architecture. Optics are not just for switching but for processor-to-memory communication.
97	*   **Concept:** Companies like **Lightmatter** and **Ayar Labs** utilize interposers to connect XPUs optically.
98	*   **Investment Implications:** This threatens standard SerDes providers and creates a new class of "optical compute" companies. Lightmatter’s recent $4.4B valuation underscores the market's belief in this architecture.[14]
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 5. Supply Chain Vertical: The Architects (Switch Silicon & DSP)
103	
104	The "brains" of the operation. These companies define the specifications and control the high-margin silicon at the heart of the network.
105	
106	### 5.1 Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: AVGO)
107	Broadcom is the undisputed heavyweight champion of networking silicon. Their "Tomahawk" line of switch ASICs is the industry standard for hyperscalers.
108	*   **CPO Leadership:** Broadcom is aggressively driving CPO to defend its switch dominance. Their "Bailly" CPO switch (51.2T) integrates their silicon photonics engines directly. By controlling the switch, the DSP, and the optics, Broadcom captures the entire value chain.
109	*   **Technical Execution:** Broadcom has demonstrated 512-lane CPO systems with high reliability, releasing shock/vibe and thermal cycling data to assuage hyperscaler fears.[15]
110	*   **Strategic Positioning:** Broadcom effectively acts as the "arms dealer" for AI. Whether the market uses pluggables or CPO, Broadcom wins via its switch silicon. However, CPO allows them to capture the ~$5000+ of optical content per switch that previously went to transceiver vendors.
111	
112	### 5.2 Marvell Technology (NASDAQ: MRVL)
113	Marvell acts as the primary counterweight to Broadcom, offering a merchant alternative with a focus on DSP excellence.
114	*   **Inphi Legacy:** The acquisition of Inphi gave Marvell best-in-class DSP technology (PAM4). Their "Nova" DSPs are critical for 1.6T optical modules.[16]
115	*   **Platform Strategy:** Marvell’s "Teralynx" switch combined with "Perseus" optical engines offers a full stack. Unlike Broadcom's more closed integrated approach, Marvell emphasizes interoperability and open standards, appealing to hyperscalers who want to avoid vendor lock-in.[17, 18]
116	*   **AI Scale-Up:** Marvell is heavily focused on the "scale-up" market—connecting GPUs within a cluster. Their optical interconnects are designed to compete with or complement proprietary fabrics like NVLink.
117	
118	### 5.3 Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA)
119	Nvidia is the system architect. While they don't manufacture the raw optical components, their architectural decisions dictate the market.
120	*   **NVLink & InfiniBand:** Nvidia's acquisition of Mellanox was a strategic move to control the interconnect. The NVLink Switch system is increasingly reliant on optical backplanes to scale beyond a single rack.
121	*   **Supply Chain Kingmaker:** Nvidia's selection of **Fabrinet** for optical assembly and **TSMC** for packaging effectively mints these companies as Tier 1 investments. The "Million-GPU" cluster roadmap necessitates a move to optical switching to manage power, keeping Nvidia at the forefront of SiPh adoption.[19, 20]
122	
123	### 5.4 Intel Corporation (NASDAQ: INTC)
124	Intel remains a complex but vital player.
125	*   **Hybrid Laser Technology:** Intel's key differentiator is its ability to bond III-V laser material onto silicon wafers at scale. This "hybrid integration" solves the light source problem better than many competitors who rely on external lasers.[6]
126	*   **Foundry Pivot:** Intel is opening its SiPh process to external customers via Intel Foundry. While the company faces broader financial headwinds, its photonics IP remains a crown jewel. The restructuring of Intel Foundry into an independent subsidiary may unlock value here, allowing it to serve competitors to its own product groups.[21, 22]
127	
128	---
129	
130	## 6. Supply Chain Vertical: The Foundries (Manufacturing the Light)
131	
132	Manufacturing photonic chips requires different tools and processes than logic chips. The foundry market is bifurcating into specialized players and volume giants.
133	
134	### 6.1 GlobalFoundries (NASDAQ: GFS)
135	GlobalFoundries (GF) has positioned itself as the premier "More-than-Moore" foundry, with silicon photonics as a cornerstone of its strategy.
136	*   **GF Fotonix Platform:** This is a monolithic platform, meaning GF manufactures the optical components (waveguides, modulators) and the RF/CMOS control electronics on the *same* chip. This eliminates the need for complex wire-bonding between a photonics die and an electronics die, improving performance and reliability.[23, 24]
137	*   **300mm Scale:** GF is the only foundry offering this monolithic capability on 300mm wafers at high volume. This scale is critical for lowering costs to levels acceptable for mass AI deployment.
138	*   **Ecosystem:** GF has built a robust ecosystem (EDA partners like Cadence/Synopsys, packaging partners) to lower the barrier to entry for fabless design houses.[24]
139	
140	### 6.2 Tower Semiconductor (NASDAQ: TSEM)
141	Tower is a specialty foundry that punches above its weight in photonics.
142	*   **PH18 Platform:** Tower’s PH18 silicon photonics process is an open platform widely used by leading transceiver makers (like InnoLight). They specialize in high-performance modulators.[25]
143	*   **Strategic Expansion:** Tower has aggressively moved into 300mm production to support the volume demands of 1.6T and 3.2T transceivers. Their partnership with Intel in New Mexico provides them with 300mm capacity without the massive capital expenditure of building a new shell.[26, 27]
144	*   **Recent Wins:** Tower recently announced volume production of 1.6T products with a Tier 1 customer, signaling their readiness for the next upgrade cycle.[28]
145	
146	### 6.3 TSMC (NYSE: TSM)
147	The world's largest foundry is the linchpin for the most advanced integration: Co-Packaged Optics.
148	*   **COUPE (Compact Universal Photonic Engine):** TSMC's proprietary technology for integrating photonics with logic. It leverages their SoIC-X (System on Integrated Chips) 3D stacking technology to place the electrical IC directly on top of the photonic IC.
149	*   **Roadmap:** TSMC is verifying 2nd-generation COUPE for 2026 production. As the manufacturer of Nvidia's GPUs and Broadcom's switches, TSMC is the natural integration point for CPO. If optics go "on-package," TSMC captures the packaging value that previously went to module assemblers.[29]
150	
151	---
152	
153	## 7. Supply Chain Vertical: The Module Makers & OSATs (Packaging the Light)
154	
155	This segment is currently realizing the most immediate revenue from the AI boom, but faces the most long-term disruption from CPO.
156	
157	### 7.1 Fabrinet (NYSE: FN)
158	Fabrinet is unique. It is not a designer but a specialized contract manufacturer for high-complexity optical/electro-mechanical packaging.
159	*   **The Nvidia Proxy:** Fabrinet manufactures the optical interconnects for Nvidia's systems. This relationship is a primary driver of their recent growth, with datacom revenue exploding. Nvidia and Cisco combined account for ~46% of revenue.[30, 31]
160	*   **Moat:** Optical alignment requires extreme precision (sub-micron). While automation is increasing, Fabrinet's skilled workforce in Thailand provides a cost and quality advantage that is hard to replicate. They are the "safe pair of hands" for high-value optical assembly.
161	*   **Financials:** Revenue for FY2025 reached $3.42 billion, with consistent earnings beats. They are a cash-rich, low-debt company serving the hottest customers in tech.[32]
162	
163	### 7.2 InnoLight Technology (300308.SZ) & Eoptolink (300502.SZ)
164	These Chinese companies are the volume leaders in the global transceiver market.
165	*   **Market Dominance:** Together, they control an estimated 60% of the 800G optical module market.[10] They are known for aggressive pricing and rapid time-to-market, often qualifying new speeds with hyperscalers (Google, Amazon) faster than Western competitors.
166	*   **Risks:** Geopolitical risk is high. U.S. tariffs or entity list restrictions could severely impact their ability to serve Western hyperscalers. Both are expanding manufacturing into Southeast Asia (Thailand) to mitigate this.[33]
167	
168	### 7.3 Amkor Technology (NASDAQ: AMKR)
169	Amkor is a top-tier OSAT (Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test) expanding into photonics.
170	*   **Advanced Packaging:** As optics move from the faceplate (pluggables) to the package (CPO), the value shifts to players like Amkor who can handle 2.5D/3D integration.
171	*   **Lightmatter Partnership:** Amkor is building the world's largest 3D photonics package for Lightmatter. This involves a massive multi-reticle die complex on an organic substrate, showcasing Amkor's ability to handle wafer-scale photonics.[34]
172	*   **US Footprint:** Amkor's new Arizona facility (supported by Apple and CHIPS Act funding) positions them as a secure, domestic packaging hub for critical AI silicon.[35]
173	
174	### 7.4 Coherent (NYSE: COHR) & Lumentum (NASDAQ: LITE)
175	The diversified optical giants.
176	*   **Coherent:** A vertically integrated powerhouse. They produce the InP wafers, the lasers, and the finished transceiver modules. This vertical control allows them to manage margins better than pure assemblers.[25]
177	*   **Lumentum:** A leader in telecom and datacom optics. Their strategic opportunity lies in **External Laser Sources (ELS)**. In CPO architectures, the laser is often kept *outside* the hot switch package to prevent thermal degradation. Lumentum is positioning itself as the primary supplier of these ELS modules, ensuring they have a role in the CPO future.[25, 36]
178	
179	---
180	
181	## 8. Supply Chain Vertical: Emerging Disruptors & Deep Tech
182	
183	This is the high-risk, high-reward frontier. These companies are betting on new architectures to solve the bottlenecks that standard silicon photonics cannot.
184	
185	### 8.1 Ayar Labs
186	A private unicorn backed by Nvidia and Intel Capital.
187	*   **Technology:** Ayar Labs replaces the electrical SerDes on a processor with an optical I/O chiplet ("TeraPHY"). This allows the processor to communicate with high bandwidth at very low power (<5pJ/bit) over distances up to 2km.
188	*   **Implication:** This enables "disaggregated computing"—separating memory from compute and pooling it. This solves the "memory wall" in AI training.[1, 37]
189	
190	### 8.2 Lightmatter
191	*   **Photonic Computing:** Lightmatter uses light not just for transport, but for calculation (matrix multiplication). Their "Passage" interconnect is a wafer-scale programmable optical fabric.
192	*   **Valuation:** Recently raised $400M at a $4.4B valuation, creating a massive war chest to scale their technology. They are positioning themselves as a platform company for the post-Moore's Law era.[14, 38]
193	
194	### 8.3 POET Technologies (NASDAQ: POET)
195	*   **The Optical Interposer:** POET's innovation is a "hybrid integration" platform. Instead of trying to force lasers onto silicon (which is hard), they use a novel interposer material that allows them to "flip-chip" lasers and detectors with passive alignment.
196	*   **Cost Advantage:** This eliminates the expensive active alignment process required by traditional assembly, potentially lowering the cost of optical engines by 20-40%. They have demonstrated 800G and 1.6T engines and are targeting the AI market.[39, 40]
197	
198	### 8.4 Lightwave Logic (NASDAQ: LWLG)
199	*   **Polymer Modulators:** Silicon modulators have physical speed limits. Lightwave Logic develops electro-optic polymers that can switch faster (100GHz+) with less voltage.
200	*   **The Thesis:** As lane speeds hit 200G and 400G, silicon may run out of steam. Polymers could be the material that extends the roadmap. However, integrating organic polymers into standard fab processes is a significant hurdle.[41]
201	
202	---
203	
204	## 9. Supply Chain Vertical: Equipment & EDA (The Enablers)
205	
206	You cannot build what you cannot design or test. This often-overlooked segment provides the picks and shovels for the photonics gold rush.
207	
208	### 9.1 Equipment: Besi & ficonTEC
209	*   **Besi (BE Semiconductor):** Holds a dominant 75% market share in advanced die attach equipment. Their hybrid bonding tools are essential for the 3D stacking used in TSMC's COUPE and other CPO processes.[42]
210	*   **ficonTEC:** A specialized German firm (private) that builds the automated assembly lines for photonics. Their machines handle the sub-micron alignment of fibers to chips—the most critical step in manufacturing.[43, 44]
211	
212	### 9.2 Test & Measurement: Keysight & FormFactor
213	*   **The Challenge:** How do you test a chip that uses light? You can't just touch a metal probe to a pad. You have to couple light in and out.
214	*   **Solution:** Keysight (electronics/optics test) and FormFactor (wafer probing) have partnered to create integrated SiPh test stations. These automated systems are critical for yield management as volumes ramp.[45, 46]
215	
216	### 9.3 EDA: Synopsys & Cadence
217	*   **Design Tools:** Designing photonics requires simulating light physics (Maxwell's equations) alongside electrical circuits. Synopsys (with Ansys) and Cadence have monopolized the EDA flow for photonics. Every chip designed by Nvidia, Broadcom, or Marvell pays a toll to these duopolists.[47, 48]
218	
219	---
220	
221	## 10. Geopolitical & Macro Risks
222	
223	### 10.1 The China Factor
224	The current supply chain is dangerously concentrated. A vast majority of the world's optical transceivers are assembled in China by InnoLight, Eoptolink, and others.
225	*   **Risk:** If the U.S. government restricts the import of Chinese optics (similar to Huawei restrictions) or restricts the export of advanced DSPs to these entities, the supply chain would fracture.
226	*   **Mitigation:** Investors should look for companies with diversified footprints. **Fabrinet** (Thailand), **Amkor** (Vietnam/US), and **GlobalFoundries** (US/Singapore/Germany) are beneficiaries of this "China Plus One" strategy.[5]
227	
228	### 10.2 The CPO "Osborne Effect"
229	The industry is currently buying 800G pluggables in massive volumes. However, the looming promise of CPO (more efficient, denser) could cause a pause in spending or "inventory digestion" as hyperscalers wait for the next generation. Companies heavily exposed to pluggables (InnoLight, Coherent) face a long-term terminal value risk if they do not successfully pivot to supplying CPO components (like ELS).
230	
231	### 10.3 Technical Execution
232	Integrating lasers on silicon is arguably one of the hardest problems in hardware engineering. Thermal mismatch, yield loss, and reliability are constant battles. Any high-profile failure of a CPO deployment (e.g., widespread field failures of an optical switch) could set the industry back years and chill investment.
233	
234	---
235	
236	## 11. Investment Synthesis and Conclusion
237	
238	The transition from copper to silicon photonics is one of the most certain structural trends in technology. The physics of AI scaling dictate it. For the investment analyst, the key is to separate the hype from the deployable reality and to identify where the value accrues in the stack.
239	
240	### Tiered Investment Framework
241	
242	| Tier | Investment Profile | Top Picks | Rationale |
243	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
244	| **Tier 1: The Core Foundation** | **Defensive Growth** | **Broadcom (AVGO)** | Captures value across the entire stack (Switch + DSP + Optics). The safest way to play the trend. |
245	| **Tier 2: The Direct AI Play** | **Aggressive Growth** | **Fabrinet (FN)** | The manufacturing engine for Nvidia. Direct correlation to AI hardware volume with a strong competitive moat. |
246	| **Tier 3: The Enablers** | **Cyclical/Industrial** | **GlobalFoundries (GFS), Besi (BESI)** | The manufacturing and equipment backbone. GFS is a strategic sovereignty play; Besi is a monopoly on the necessary packaging tools. |
247	| **Tier 4: The Speculative Upside** | **Venture/High-Beta** | **POET Technologies (POET), Lightwave Logic (LWLG)** | High risk, but massive potential if their specific architectures (Interposer/Polymer) are adopted as industry standards. |
248	
249	**Conclusion:** The "Copper Wall" is real, and the bridge across it is built of silicon photonics. The supply chain has matured from a scientific curiosity to a critical industrial ecosystem. As 2025 approaches, the market is shifting from early adoption to mass deployment, creating a prime window for capital allocation into the leaders of this optical revolution. The winners will be those who can manufacture light at the scale of silicon.
250	
251	---
