9	
10	# The Photonic Pivot: Structural Architecture Shifts, Co-Packaged Optics Adoption, and Investment Outcomes in the AI Infrastructure Supercycle
11	
12	## Executive Summary of the Photonic Transition
13	
14	The artificial intelligence hardware ecosystem has reached a definitive, irreversible architectural inflection point in the first quarter of 2026. The historical scaling laws that have dictated data center performance for the past two decades—relying predominantly on faster graphics processing units, higher SerDes (Serializer/Deserializer) transmission rates, and increasingly aggressive printed circuit board designs—have violently collided with the absolute physical limits of electrical engineering.[1] As hyperscale artificial intelligence clusters push interconnect requirements beyond 100 Terabits per second per node, traditional copper interconnects are failing to deliver the required bandwidth-per-watt due to catastrophic signal degradation, dielectric loss, and thermal throttling.[1, 2] Consequently, the global semiconductor industry is executing a forced, massive paradigm shift from electrical to optical signaling, positioning silicon photonics and co-packaged optics as the foundational bedrock of next-generation gigascale artificial intelligence factories.[3, 4]
15	
16	This exhaustive analysis investigates the optical interconnect supercycle currently dominating the semiconductor capital markets. It evaluates the fundamental physics and unit economics driving the rapid adoption of 1.6T optical modules, the structural and packaging transition to Co-Packaged Optics, and the profound investment outcomes for critical supply chain constituents. Specifically, this report dissects the financial trajectories and strategic positioning of Coherent Corp., Lumentum Holdings, Applied Optoelectronics, and IQE plc following unprecedented capital interventions by major ecosystem players.[5, 6, 7, 8, 9] Furthermore, the analysis evaluates the frontier thesis of purely photonic computing, critically assessing the bull case for matrix multiplication operating at the speed of light as proposed by market speculators and early-stage ventures.[10] The findings indicate that while pure photonic processing remains a highly speculative, forward-looking venture fraught with digital-to-analog conversion bottlenecks, the transition to optical data movement is a structural certainty that is completely rewriting the capital expenditure and operational expenditure profiles of the global computing infrastructure.[2]
17	
18	## The Physics of the Interconnect Bottleneck
19	
20	To comprehend the sudden and massive capital reallocation toward photonics in 2026, one must first examine the physical degradation of legacy electrical architectures under the strain of modern computational workloads. In legacy data center architectures, computational performance scaled largely independently of networking constraints. The internal logic speed of the processor was the primary gating factor for system output.[2] However, the advent of massive large language models and mixture-of-experts architectures requires tens of thousands of processors to operate as a single, tightly coupled logical computer.[1, 11] These distributed systems generate petabytes of "east-west" traffic—data moving horizontally between servers rather than vertically to the internet—daily, demanding extreme bandwidth density and vanishingly low latency.[11]
21	
22	### Dielectric Loss and the Inescapable Power Wall
23	
24	Research presented at the IEEE International Solid-State Circuits Conference demonstrates conclusively that dielectric loss in traditional copper interconnects at high frequencies has hit a physical and thermodynamic brick wall.[2] As electrical bandwidth increases to meet the demands of advanced accelerators, insertion loss and crosstalk rise non-linearly with both frequency and physical distance.[1] To compensate for this rapid signal degradation over copper traces, hardware engineers are forced to implement increasingly aggressive equalization protocols. This involves deploying complex physical layer implementations utilizing Continuous Time Linear Equalization, Decision Feedback Equalization, and highly power-hungry retimers and digital signal processors simply to recover signal integrity.[1]
25	
26	The immediate second-order effect of this electrical compensation is an unsustainable surge in power consumption. In legacy systems, data transmission consumes up to thirty percent of total system power, a disproportionate volume of capital incinerated purely on moving data rather than computing it.[2] Adding more processors no longer scales linearly; the power, cooling, and infrastructure costs required to facilitate communication rise vastly faster than the computational performance itself.[1] The industry refers to this phenomenon as the power wall, a hard constraint that renders traditional pluggable copper modules physically obsolete for the 102.4T switching fabrics currently being deployed by entities like NVIDIA and Broadcom.[3] Furthermore, distance itself has become a critical liability, as energy per bit and signal integrity degrade sharply with every additional centimeter of electrical reach.[1]
27	
28	### The Photonic Imperative and Silicon Integration
29	
30	Photonics circumvents these fundamental physical limitations by utilizing the properties of light rather than voltage to transmit data. Optical links maintain massive bandwidth over centimeter- to meter-scale distances with near-zero signal loss and entirely bypass the need for heavy, power-consuming electrical equalization overhead.[1] By transitioning from electrons to photons, data center architects can fundamentally decouple physical distance from bandwidth degradation, thereby redefining system topologies and allowing for highly flexible, disaggregated server designs.[1] The battleground for artificial intelligence supremacy in 2026 is no longer the internal logic speed of the processor, but the input/output bandwidth and power efficiency of chip-to-chip and rack-to-rack communication.[2]
31	
32	## The 1.6T Supercycle and Silicon Photonics Penetration
33	
34	The current calendar year marks the critical infrastructure pivot where light-based interconnects officially dethrone electrical wiring in high-performance computing.[3] This transition is manifesting in two distinct but technologically complementary waves: the immediate 1.6T pluggable module supercycle and the structural, longer-term transition to Co-Packaged Optics architectures.
35	
36	The technical backbone of the immediate 2026 infrastructure surge is the 1.6T optical module. Doubling the bandwidth of the previous 800G standard, the 1.6T module is not merely a speed upgrade but an absolute survival necessity for the world's most advanced gigascale factories.[3] Market analyses, including landmark reporting from Nomura, indicate that the 1.6T module market is experiencing an unprecedented supercycle. Global shipments of these advanced modules are projected to explode from a baseline of 2.5 million units in the previous year to a staggering 20 million units by the end of 2026.[3]
37	
38	Crucially, the underlying architecture inside these optical modules is experiencing a foundational shift. While previous generations of optical transceivers relied heavily on discrete Indium Phosphide lasers or Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Lasers, 2026 is defined by the rapid dominance of Silicon Photonics.[3] Silicon Photonics leverages mature, existing CMOS manufacturing processes—the exact same fabrication techniques used to build traditional microchips—to integrate complex optical functions onto a single silicon die.[3] This integration drastically reduces manufacturing complexities, improves reliability, and drives down unit costs at scale. Consequently, Silicon Photonics penetration is projected to hit between fifty and seventy percent within the high-end 1.6T segment this year.[3] Corroborating this shift, TrendForce research estimates that the global shipment share of 800G and 1.6T optical transceiver modules will climb from roughly nineteen percent in 2024 to over sixty percent by 2026, cementing high-speed optics as the default standard for artificial intelligence data centers.[12]
39	
40	## Co-Packaged Optics: Redefining the Computing Unit
41	
42	While 1.6T pluggable modules address the immediate, pressing bandwidth needs of current-generation clusters, Co-Packaged Optics represents the long-term architectural endgame for the semiconductor industry. Co-Packaged Optics fundamentally rewrites the unit economics of the data center by pulling the electro-optical conversion module off the edge of the printed circuit board and placing it directly onto the compute package, immediately adjacent to the switch application-specific integrated circuit or the graphics processing unit itself.[2, 13]
43	
44	This direct package-level integration eradicates the severe electrical losses associated with traversing long copper traces across a motherboard. By moving the optical engine onto the package, engineering teams can drop the power required for a communication link from roughly thirty watts down to merely nine watts.[1] This disruptive technology is not merely a physical upgrade; it is the critical economic lever for driving down Operational Expenditures and maximizing the Lifetime Value of deeply expensive server clusters.[2]
45	
46	| Architectural Evaluation Metric | Traditional Pluggable Optics | Co-Packaged Optics | Optical Circuit Switches |
47	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
48	| **Data Conversion Location** | Edge of the PCB (Panel) | Directly on the Compute Package | All-optical (no intermediate conversion) |
49	| **Signal Degradation Profile** | High (Copper trace dependency) | Minimal (Direct optical escape) | Negligible (Direct fiber-to-fiber) |
50	| **Power Consumption per Link** | ~30 Watts | ~9 Watts | ~100W per entire switch array |
51	| **Primary System Use Case** | Legacy Data Centers, 400G/800G | High-density Clusters, >1.6T | Inter-rack Data Transmission |
52	| **Projected Adoption Timeline** | Currently peaking / Plateauing | Scaling exponentially (2026-2030) | Mass deployment (2025-2026) |
53	
54	The adoption timeline for Co-Packaged Optics has accelerated significantly faster than historical forecasts predicted. Industry analyses now project exponential growth in deployments, with 3.2T co-packaged ports expected to exceed ten million units by 2029.[1] Furthermore, market intelligence firm LightCounting anticipates that sales of lasers and photonic integrated circuits for optical transceivers will grow from $2.4 billion in 2023 to $5.9 billion by 2029, fueled almost entirely by the insatiable demands of artificial intelligence workloads.[1]
55	
56	By utilizing Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express optical protocols, Co-Packaged Optics effectively turns entire boards and server racks into what architects refer to as extended packages.[1] In this new paradigm, traditional copper is relegated exclusively to localized, in-package communication over distances of mere millimeters. Conversely, optics take over all package-to-package, board-level, and rack-scale distances.[1] One prominent industry example of this shift is Ayar Labs' optical interconnect chiplet, TeraPHY, which delivers eight terabits per second of bandwidth directly from the package edge.[1] When photonics is integrated directly into the package, the printed circuit board ceases to be the system bottleneck, enabling a completely topology-agnostic approach to data center design where modules can be placed precisely where thermal and architectural efficiency demands.[1]
57	
58	### Advanced Packaging and the Testing Ecosystem
59	
60	The successful deployment of Co-Packaged Optics relies entirely on advancements in semiconductor packaging and testing methodologies. Traditional pluggable optical modules are increasingly constrained by signal loss precisely because they require long electrical traces, but Co-Packaged Optics overcomes this by utilizing advanced packaging technologies to enable high-density integration of photonic and electronic integrated circuits.[13] This requires highly sophisticated manufacturing techniques, most notably TSMC's SoIC (System on Integrated Chips) platform and bumpless hybrid bonding.[13] These processes enable the vertical stacking of logic dies and heterogeneous components at sub-ten-micron pitches, dramatically shortening interconnect lengths and reducing resistance.[13]
61	
62	As packaging complexity increases, so too does the need for rigorous, high-volume testing infrastructure. This dynamic is perfectly illustrated by the surge in demand for specialized silicon photonics test systems. For example, Aehr Test Systems announced in March 2026 a significant follow-on purchase order for its fully automated wafer-level burn-in equipment.[14] Their FOX-XP systems are capable of testing up to nine 300-millimeter wafers in parallel, a critical capability for ensuring the reliability of silicon photonics dies before they are irreversibly bonded to expensive graphics processing units in a Co-Packaged Optics arrangement.[14] The integration of these advanced testing frameworks proves that the entire supply chain is maturing rapidly to support mass commercialization.
63	
64	## The All-Optical Network and Optical Circuit Switches
65	
66	While Co-Packaged Optics solves the immediate server-level interconnect power problem, scaling at the macro rack and cluster level requires a completely novel switching topology. Historically, data centers utilized electrical switches in rigid spine-and-leaf configurations. This required optical signals traveling between racks to be converted back into electrical signals at the switch, processed and routed, and then converted back into light for the next leg of the journey.[12] This continuous optical-electrical-optical conversion is responsible for massive latency overhead and exorbitant power drain.[12]
67	
68	In 2026, architectures like Google's next-generation tensor processing unit, Ironwood, are pioneering the use of true all-optical networks, specifically deploying the Apollo Optical Circuit Switch.[12] The Apollo system utilizes complex Micro-Electromechanical Systems, essentially arrays of microscopic mirrors, to physically bounce light between incoming and outgoing fiber optic cables, enabling direct fiber-to-fiber routing.[12] Because the signal never leaves the photonic domain, power consumption collapses entirely. A single Optical Circuit Switch consumes merely one hundred watts, representing an astonishing ninety-five percent reduction in power consumption compared to traditional electrical switches that draw roughly three thousand watts.[12]
69	
70	Furthermore, these optical switches possess a profound, long-term economic advantage: they are inherently protocol and data-rate agnostic. Upgrading a data center's bandwidth from 800G to 1.6T, and eventually to 3.2T, requires only swapping out the edge optical modules on the servers; the central mirror array within the switch remains perfectly capable of routing the higher-capacity light, significantly lowering long-term architectural upgrade capital expenditures.[12] With industry projections indicating that nearly four million Google tensor processing units will be shipped in 2026, the baseline demand for 800G and 1.6T optical modules is expected to exceed six million units purely to populate this specific all-optical ecosystem.[12]
71	
72	## NVIDIA's $4 Billion Strategic Supply Chain Intervention
73	
74	The most definitive and aggressive market validation of the optical interconnect thesis occurred in early March 2026, when NVIDIA announced a massive $4 billion combined strategic investment into the optical component supply chain, split evenly between Coherent Corp. and Lumentum Holdings.[4, 15, 16, 17] This deliberate dual-vendor maneuver mirrors NVIDIA's previous aggressive supply chain interventions designed to monopolize High Bandwidth Memory and advanced packaging capacity during the initial stages of the artificial intelligence boom.[15]
75	
76	By explicitly identifying optical components—specifically Indium Phosphide lasers and silicon photonics—as the emerging gating factor on continued infrastructure scaling, NVIDIA has shifted its posture from acting as a mere procurer of components to acting as the central bank and primary financier for the global optical ecosystem.[15, 16]
77	
78	The structure of these strategic partnerships is comprehensive and multi-layered. First, NVIDIA executed a direct capital injection, investing $2 billion into each company, structured as convertible preferred stock, to support immediate research and development and to fund future operational capacity.[4, 16, 17, 18, 19] Second, the agreements include multibillion-dollar purchase commitments alongside guaranteed future capacity access rights.[4, 15, 17, 19] This effectively locks out competing hyperscalers and chip designers from utilizing the most advanced optical fabrication lines over the coming years, securing NVIDIA's dominance over the physical networking layer.[15]
79	
80	### Geopolitical Resilience and Domestic Fabrication
81	
82	A critical caveat and central objective of this investment is the massive expansion of United States-based manufacturing capabilities. Lumentum is explicitly utilizing the capital injection to construct a state-of-the-art wafer fabrication facility within the United States, while Coherent is significantly adding to its existing domestic footprint.[4, 15, 16, 17, 19, 20] 
83	
84	This explicit mandate highlights a critical third-order insight: supply chain geographic resilience is now viewed by leading technology firms as equally vital as technological superiority. By localizing the manufacturing of advanced laser components and silicon photonics, NVIDIA and its partners mitigate the severe geopolitical risks and potential tariff exposures associated with legacy Asian optical supply chains.[16, 21] This localized fabrication ensures an uninterrupted, secure domestic supply of the critical lasers required for future Co-Packaged Optics deployment, safeguarding the buildout of gigawatt-scale factories regardless of global trade disruptions.[4, 16]
85	
86	Both companies are heavily integrated into NVIDIA's existing platforms. The proprietary Spectrum-X Ethernet networking switches, which are critical for scaling artificial intelligence workloads and have been adopted by hyperscalers including Meta and Oracle, heavily utilize Lumentum's advanced lasers alongside Coherent's silicon photonics.[20, 22] The capital injection ensures that the raw material pipeline for these switching fabrics will not run dry as global deployments accelerate.[20, 22]
87	
88	## Investment Outcomes and Capital Market Dynamics
89	
90	The stark realization that the next phase of the computing boom is being fought in the highly specialized trenches of laser technology, advanced packaging, and silicon photonics has triggered a ferocious repricing of optical assets across global public markets.[16] Capital markets, which previously over-indexed heavily on end-user software applications and pure-play graphics processing unit designers, have aggressively rotated capital into the underlying physical infrastructure required to sustain the industry's scaling laws.[2]
91	
92	### The S&P 500 Vanguard: Lumentum and Coherent
93	
94	The immediate and most visible beneficiaries of this macroeconomic rotation and NVIDIA’s direct intervention are Lumentum Holdings and Coherent Corp. Both organizations have fundamentally graduated from being viewed as cyclical, mid-cap networking component suppliers to being recognized as foundational pillars of the global technology index.
95	
96	In a move that vividly signals the structural embedding of the artificial infrastructure trade into the broader American economy, both Lumentum and Coherent, alongside data center power infrastructure provider Vertiv, were officially added to the S&P 500 index during the March 2026 quarterly rebalancing.[23] This index inclusion is not merely a reputational milestone; it is a massive structural buying event. With over $7 trillion benchmarked to the S&P 500, every passive index fund and exchange-traded fund tracking the index is systematically forced to purchase shares of these companies, creating a wave of price-insensitive buying that establishes a permanent floor under their valuations.[23] Similarly, to fill the vacancies left by their graduation, companies like Solstice Advanced Materials and precision timing firm SiTime were added to the S&P MidCap 400, illustrating how the entire materials and timing supply chain is being pulled upward by the optical supercycle.[24]
97	
98	Lumentum has emerged as an unstoppable force in high-value laser manufacturing. Following the investment news, the stock's valuation skyrocketed, trading near $558.44 and marking an extraordinary 814 percent increase over the trailing twelve months, with a year-to-date gain exceeding fifty-one percent.[23] Fundamentally, Lumentum's fiscal second-quarter 2026 results demonstrated massive operational leverage and pristine execution. The company reported over sixty-five percent year-over-year revenue growth, reaching $665.5 million, and expanded its non-GAAP operating margin by an astonishing 1700 basis points to reach 25.2 percent, delivering non-GAAP diluted net income of $1.67 per share.[6] 
99	
100	A central driver of this margin expansion is the market shift toward higher-value 200G per lane electro-absorption modulated lasers, which command approximately twice the average selling price of legacy 100G products.[25] Furthermore, Lumentum's optical circuit switch backlog surpassed $400 million across three major customers, addressing an optical circuit switch market that analysts have recently, and violently, revised upward from a $500 million estimate to nearly $1.5 billion for 2026.[25] The strategic acquisition of Cloud Light also proved highly accretive, with transceiver sales from that unit increasing by $50 million quarter-over-quarter, prompting analysts to project Cloud Light sales could exceed $1.3 billion by fiscal year 2027.[25] 
101	
102	Coherent has similarly experienced tremendous equity appreciation, with shares surging over 161 percent over the past year and demonstrating particularly strong momentum leading into March 2026.[25] In its first-quarter fiscal 2026 report, Coherent posted exceptional revenue of $1.58 billion, representing a nineteen percent year-over-year increase on a pro forma basis, driven almost entirely by relentless demand from datacenters.[7] The company generated strong free cash flow, allowing it to pay down $400 million of its debt and refinance its remaining obligations, significantly strengthening its balance sheet ahead of the Co-Packaged Optics deployment wave.[7] Analysts estimate the total addressable market for Co-Packaged Optics lasers will reach approximately $22 billion by 2030, and Coherent’s established leadership in 800G revenue and 1.6T datacom transceivers positions the company to capture disproportionate market share.[25] 
103	
104	### High-Beta Alpha: Applied Optoelectronics
105	
106	While Coherent and Lumentum represent large-cap, diversified, index-anchored plays, Applied Optoelectronics has firmly established itself as the high-beta, pure-play transceiver rocket ship of the current market cycle. Shares of Applied Optoelectronics soared 22.4 percent immediately following the broader sector news regarding NVIDIA's supply chain interventions, leading the company to an incredible 173.49 percent year-to-date gain heading into early March 2026.[5, 16]
107	
108	The financial trajectory of Applied Optoelectronics perfectly illustrates the raw, unfiltered velocity of the 1.6T transceiver supercycle. After posting record fourth-quarter 2025 revenue of $134.27 million—up 33.9 percent year-over-year—management issued forward guidance that fundamentally reset institutional expectations.[5] The company confidently projects that full-year 2026 revenue will easily exceed the $1 billion threshold, generating non-GAAP operating profit above $120 million.[5, 21, 26, 27] 
109	
110	However, the most staggering data point resides in the company's 2027 modeling. By the second quarter of 2027, management forecasts monthly transceiver revenue potential of $378 million.[5, 21, 26] This astronomical monthly run-rate is meticulously broken down across technological nodes: $90 million derived from legacy 100G and 400G products, $217 million from the currently dominant 800G standard, and an impressive $71 million strictly from the bleeding-edge 1.6T segment.[21] To facilitate this demand, the company expanded its capital expenditures drastically, incinerating $209 million for the full year, significantly exceeding previous projections of $120 million to $150 million.[21]
111	
112	A critical strategic advantage for Applied Optoelectronics, perfectly aligning with the geopolitical thesis driving NVIDIA's investments, is its established United States manufacturing footprint. As global trade tensions persist and tariff threats continuously loom over the semiconductor sector, Applied Optoelectronics management has explicitly highlighted that their domestic production lines insulate their products from future tariff risks, an invaluable selling point to hyperscalers demanding absolute supply chain security.[21] For Applied Optoelectronics, the primary constraint is no longer market demand—which is effectively infinite for the foreseeable future—but solely its own internal production capacity and supply chain throughput capabilities.[21]
113	
114	| Optical Equity Profile | Foundational Role in AI Ecosystem | Fiscal/Calendar 2026 Financial Projections | Strategic Catalysts and Market Positioning |
115	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
116	| **Lumentum Holdings** | Advanced lasers, Optical Circuit Switches, and CPO systems | $665.5M Q2 FY26 (>65% YoY growth); 25.2% Op Margin | $2B NVIDIA investment; S&P 500 Addition; OCS Backlog >$400M |
117	| **Coherent Corp.** | Silicon photonics, 1.6T transceivers, customized DSPs | $1.58B Q1 FY26 (19% pro forma growth); Debt Reduction | $2B NVIDIA investment; S&P 500 Addition; CPO Leadership |
118	| **Applied Optoelectronics** | Pure-play advanced optical transceivers (800G/1.6T) | Targeting >$1B for 2026; $378M/mo run-rate by mid-2027 | Insulated US manufacturing base; Massive 800G/1.6T demand ramp |
119	| **IQE plc** | Epitaxial wafers and foundational compound substrates | Upper end of £97M FY25; positive £2M EBITDA | Active Strategic Review (M&A target); Surging Indium Phosphide demand |
120	
121	### The Substrate Foundation: IQE plc
122	
123	Moving further upstream in the complex optical supply chain lies IQE plc, a leading global supplier of compound semiconductor wafer products and advanced epitaxial solutions.[9, 28] IQE manufactures the fundamental Indium Phosphide and Gallium Arsenide materials required to grow the highly complex lasers utilized by downstream integrators like Lumentum and Coherent.[29]
124	
125	IQE's trading updates entering 2026 indicate robust, recovering fundamental health driven directly by artificial intelligence infrastructure demands. For the full year 2025, IQE reported revenue performing at the absolute upper end of its guidance, landing at approximately £97 million, with adjusted EBITDA successfully flipping to a positive £2.0 million.[8, 9, 28, 29] This financial recovery was supported by higher capacity utilization and vastly improved operating leverage.[8, 9] The company entered 2026 with a highly visible and strong first-quarter order book, heavily supported by higher-than-forecast photonics demand stemming directly from data center buildouts and AI-enabled compute platforms, alongside ongoing strength in the consumer mobile sector.[8, 9, 29, 30] Furthermore, IQE benefited from faster-than-expected funding releases for United States military and defense programs, highlighting the dual-use nature of advanced compound semiconductors.[8, 9, 29] The firm's liquidity profile stabilized, ending 2025 with £15.6 million in cash and securing vital covenant waivers from its primary lender, HSBC Bank plc.[9, 29, 31]
126	
127	However, the most compelling investment angle regarding IQE in early 2026 is corporate action. The company is actively undergoing a comprehensive Strategic Review, publicly confirming that the board is negotiating non-binding offers for the entire Group as well as evaluating separate, distinct bids for specific component assets.[28, 29, 32] Given the extreme strategic importance of compound semiconductor epitaxy to the global supply chain, and the intense desire for vertical integration demonstrated by entities like NVIDIA, IQE represents a highly attractive, foundational acquisition target. The consolidation of upstream materials is a natural consequence of the optical supercycle, as downstream players seek to secure the raw components necessary to feed the insatiable 1.6T and Co-Packaged Optics manufacturing lines.
128	
129	## The Speculative Frontier: Photonic Computing vs. Optical Interconnects
130	
131	As global capital markets deeply digest the absolute necessity of optical interconnects—the technology dedicated strictly to moving data with light—a highly speculative, forward-looking debate has simultaneously emerged regarding optical computation, which involves utilizing light to fundamentally perform the mathematical logic itself.
132	
133	The bull case for purely photonic computing was aggressively publicized in the first quarter of 2026 by Martin Shkreli, acting in his capacity as a strategic advisor to the micro-cap enterprise Q/C Technologies Inc..[10, 33] In a widely circulated Substack essay titled "Photonic Computing: The Final AI Hardware Frontier," Shkreli articulates a theoretically sound, albeit practically distant, technological premise that challenges the prevailing dominance of electrical graphics processing units.[10]
134	
135	### The Theoretical Photonic Mathematical Advantage
136	
137	The foundational argument relies heavily on the physics of computational complexity. Modern electrical processors, such as NVIDIA's reigning architectures, spend approximately ninety-nine percent of their computational cycles performing one specific task: matrix multiplication.[10] This mathematical operation forms the bedrock of all deep learning neural networks. In traditional silicon-based environments, matrix multiplication scales at cubic time complexity, mathematically denoted as $O(N^3)$. As the matrices grow to encompass trillions of parameters, the time and energy required to compute them electrically scale exponentially.
138	
139	Photonic computing proposes bypassing this bottleneck by using the intrinsic physical properties of light—specifically wave interference patterns and rapid Fourier transforms executed via passive optical components—to compute these massive matrix multiplications inherently as the light passes through a uniquely designed photonic chip.[33, 34] In theory, light handles these complex operations in near-linear time, or even constant time $O(1)$, compared to the cubic time required by graphics processing units.[10, 33] For the massive parameter matrices required by frontier models, replacing electrical cubic time with photonic near-linear time would theoretically result in an astronomical leap in processing speed and energy efficiency, entirely upending the trillion-dollar graphics processing unit market and disrupting the foundational economics of companies like NVIDIA.[10] Shkreli explicitly positioned this optical approach against quantum computing, arguing that "frontier computing is optical, not quantum," citing validation studies from Microsoft research.[35]
140	
141	### Market Skepticism and the Digital-to-Analog Bottleneck
142	
143	Despite the theoretical elegance of photonic computing, institutional capital markets remain deeply and justifiably skeptical of its near-term viability as an existential threat to incumbent electrical architectures. Prediction markets, such as Polymarket, vividly illustrate this skepticism, continuing to price in a sixty-one percent probability that NVIDIA will easily end 2026 as the world's most valuable company by market capitalization, assuming continued graphics processing unit dominance.[10] 
144	
145	This profound market hesitation is heavily rooted in both the financial and operational realities of the specific companies attempting to commercialize the technology, as well as severe engineering roadblocks. Q/C Technologies, the firm advised by Shkreli, was originally formed as a stark pivot from a pharmaceutical and cryptocurrency infrastructure background known as TNF Pharmaceuticals in late 2025.[10] The company carries a deeply distressed financial profile, reporting a trailing twelve-month net loss of approximately $23.4 million against minimal generated revenue.[33] Despite a brief thirty-eight percent surge in its stock price following Shkreli's promotional efforts on social media platforms, the equity remains down over ninety-five percent year-to-date, reflecting a broader investor consensus that purely optical computation remains confined to nascent, laboratory phases.[33, 35] Shkreli's own controversial history, stemming from his tenure at Turing Pharmaceuticals and subsequent federal fraud charges regarding hedge fund mismanagement, further complicates institutional adoption of his highly speculative thesis.[36, 37, 38, 39]
146	
147	Beyond the corporate specifics, a critical second-order engineering insight thoroughly explains the broader market's hesitation toward photonic computation: the conversion bottleneck. While optical chips can technically perform the matrix mathematics almost instantly, the underlying data must still originate in the digital domain. It must be converted from digital electrical signals into analog optical signals via highly complex digital-to-analog converters, processed through the optical matrix, and then read back out via analog-to-digital converters to be utilized by the rest of the software stack. For current artificial intelligence workloads, the latency overhead and massive power requirements of this continuous, rapid digital-to-analog conversion process completely negate the speed advantages gained by the optical mathematics. 
148	
149	Therefore, while the premise that trillions of dollars of market value exist to make matrix calculations faster is entirely accurate, the graphics processing unit dominance—currently priced at a relatively modest twenty-five times forward earnings despite generating sixty-seven percent earnings per share growth—remains entirely secure for the duration of the 2026-2030 supercycle.[10] The immediate, monetizable frontier is not photonic computing, but the photonic delivery of data to the electrical processor.
150	
151	## Synthesis and the 2030 Architectural Outlook
152	
153	As the global semiconductor industry aggressively marches through the 2026 1.6T module supercycle, the foundational architecture is being irrevocably laid for the next half-decade of artificial intelligence hardware engineering. The successful transition from pluggable modules to widespread Co-Packaged Optics and All-Optical Networks relies on the continuous maturation of several key ecosystem components.
154	
155	The integration of computing and photonics will drive unprecedented demand for advanced packaging, specifically hybrid bonding and highly complex silicon interposers.[1, 13] As these technologies mature, design paradigms will shift entirely toward true system-technology co-optimization flows, where electrical, optical, thermal, and packaging assumptions are evaluated holistically in the early architectural phase, rather than sequentially.[1] This integrated design philosophy is essential to prevent the power delivery limits and thermal constraints that have stalled copper interconnects from reappearing in new forms.
156	
157	Standardization remains critical. The rapid adoption of Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express standards adapted specifically for optical transit ensures that the ecosystem does not fragment into proprietary silos. By preserving interoperability, hyperscalers can confidently invest billions into optical infrastructure, knowing that modules can be placed agnostically across continuous rack architectures.[1]
158	
159	The 2026 landscape is defined by the decisive, physically mandated pivot from electrical copper interconnects to advanced silicon photonics. This transition is not merely a scheduled bandwidth upgrade on a standard roadmap; it is a fundamental, existential redesign dictated by the inescapable laws of physics and thermodynamics.[3] As model parameter counts continue to swell into the multi-trillion range, the core economic viability of data centers rests entirely on mitigating power consumption and latency via Co-Packaged Optics and Optical Circuit Switches.[2, 12]
160	
161	NVIDIA's unprecedented $4 billion vertical integration play—directly capitalizing Coherent and Lumentum to secure domestic, United States-based laser fabrication—serves as the ultimate institutional market validation of this technological shift.[4, 15, 16, 17] This massive market intervention has catalyzed historic revaluations across the sector, driving Tier 1 suppliers directly into the S&P 500 while sparking explosive revenue growth trajectories for pure-play transceiver manufacturers like Applied Optoelectronics and foundational compound semiconductor substrate providers like IQE.[5, 21, 23, 29] While fringe theories regarding purely photonic computing capture speculative retail attention, the empirical realities of conversion bottlenecks relegate such architectures to a distant, secondary horizon.[10] The immediate, highly investable reality is the optical interconnect supercycle. Failing to rigorously map and invest alongside the silicon photonics supply chain in 2026 is akin to ignoring the foundational physical constraints of modern infrastructure; those positioned appropriately within the Co-Packaged Optics, Optical Circuit Switch, and 1.6T transceiver ecosystems stand to capture the most significant and durable hardware dividend of the coming decade.[2]

8	
9	# Photonics and CPO Investment Outlook
10	
11	## Original Query
12	> Summarise the perspectives of these articles with a focus on investment outcomes for Coherent, Lumentum, IQE, AAOI, and COHR. What is the implication for adoption rates for photoinics and CPO.
13	
14	https://x.com/MartinShkreli/status/2029936664429183319
15	
16	https://x.com/damnang2/status/2029104889306202420
17	
18	> What valuations multiples (Fwd P/S, P/E, EV/EBIT) are COHR, LITE, IQE, AAOI,AXTI trading on.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## The core theses on X and what the accounts are arguing
23	
24	The investment arguments circulating in this cluster of posts and the broader X photonics community during this exact window converge on several interlocking claims. First, **the copper-to-glass transition is mandatory, not optional**. At 800G speeds and beyond, copper wiring hits hard physical limits around heat, signal degradation, and power consumption — scaling to a million GPUs with pluggable transceivers would consume ~180 MW in optics alone, per NVIDIA's own math. Second, the optical supply chain is effectively **sold out through at least 2027**, creating pricing power and margin expansion for incumbents. Third, silicon photonics, CPO, and LPO (linear pluggable optics) are advancing on three simultaneous fronts, with only one core goal: eliminate energy consumption and latency to reshape pricing power in the 1.6T era. Fourth, unlike the 2000 fiber bubble, the 2026 infrastructure buildout is rigid demand forced by AI compute needs — not speculative overbuilding.
25	
26	The five accounts cover the photonics investment landscape from different vantage points. **@damnang2**, a self-described semiconductor engineer in Silicon Valley with three of the five posts, brings technical insider perspective on silicon photonics process technology and CPO architecture. @MartinShkreli, with ~398K followers and a track record of contrarian tech calls, appears to be weighing in on the sector's investability after the NVIDIA catalyst. @superposition_V's late-February post predates the NVIDIA announcement and likely represents an early-mover thesis on CPO adoption timing.
27	
28	## Conclusion: a tale of two timescales
29	
30	Three insights emerge from the data that are underappreciated. First, the **"blast radius" asymmetry** is often overlooked: while CPO has 5× fewer failures, each failure affects 64+ ports versus one for pluggables, making the net operational impact highly dependent on cluster architecture and fault tolerance design. Second, **network flattening** enabled by CPO's high-radix switches may deliver more economic value (up to 7% cluster cost reduction) than the power savings alone (~3%), making topology optimization the hidden driver of CPO economics. Third, the **448G SerDes generation expected around 2027–2028** represents a hard inflection point where signal integrity challenges may make pluggables technically unviable at the required scale, transforming CPO from an optimization choice to an architectural necessity—regardless of its yield maturity at that point.
31	
32	The quantitative picture reveals CPO as a technology with **proven reliability advantages** (5× better MTBF, zero link flaps in millions of test hours) and **transformative power efficiency** (3.5–5× improvement), constrained by **immature manufacturing yields** (60–75% versus >90% for CMOS) and an **upfront cost premium** that only reverses at hyperscale. The critical yield threshold of **>80–85%** remains 15–25 points away for most heterogeneous integration processes—roughly where CMOS was in the late 1990s, suggesting a multi-year learning curve remains.
33	
34	## Company-by-company investment positioning and what the numbers show
35	
36	**Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI)** is the consensus "dark horse" on X — the most discussed name among the @damnang2 posts. Full-year 2025 revenue hit **$455.7 million** (up 83% YoY), and management guided 2026 revenue to **exceed $1 billion** versus consensus of ~$834 million. The company's 800G ramp is expected to dominate revenue beginning Q2 2026, with production capacity targeting over 200,000 units per month by mid-2026. AAOI launched a **400mW narrow-linewidth pump laser** specifically designed for silicon photonics and CPO in December 2025, positioning it as a component supplier to CPO architectures as well as a transceiver vendor. The company is deeply bound to Microsoft (28.8% of 2025 revenue) through its cost-advantaged LPO solution, which occupies an excellent ecological niche in the mid-end market — sensitive to power consumption but not yet willing to pay for full CPO. The stock surged **22.4% on the NVIDIA announcement day despite having zero direct involvement in the deal**. Key risks include extreme customer concentration (Digicomm 53%, Microsoft 29%), heavy dilution from a $250 million ATM offering, and still-negative GAAP profitability.
37	
38	**Coherent Corp (COHR)** occupies the role of vertically integrated industrial-scale producer. With Q2 FY2026 revenue of **$1.69 billion** (up 22% pro forma), a book-to-bill ratio exceeding **4x** in data centers, and bookings visibility extending into calendar 2028, Coherent is the volume leader with roughly 25% market share in optical transceivers. The company received **$2 billion in non-dilutive capital from NVIDIA** as part of a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment for advanced laser and optical networking products, specifically targeting silicon photonics for the Spectrum-X platform. Coherent's 6-inch indium phosphide wafer ramp — with wafer starts quadrupling quarter-over-quarter — gives it a manufacturing moat. The stock was added to the S&P 500 on March 6, 2026, and trades at ~35x forward earnings after a 93% YTD run. Analyst targets range from $168 to Barclays' **$350**, raised the day after the NVIDIA deal. Initial CPO revenue is expected late 2026. The X consensus views COHR as the "strategic streamliner" — a company that sold its aerospace, defense, and materials processing divisions to concentrate resources on capturing the fattest margin in AI data center photonics.
39	
40	**Lumentum (LITE)** is positioned as the specialized performance leader. Q2 FY2026 revenue hit a record **$665.5 million** (up 65.5% YoY), with non-GAAP EPS of $1.67 beating consensus by $0.28. The company guided Q3 revenue to **$780M-$830M**, implying ~85% year-over-year growth. Lumentum is **currently the only supplier shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs at volume** — the critical component for 1.6T pluggable transceivers — and its CEO has called 2026 "a breakout year for laser chip sales." NVIDIA invested $2 billion via convertible preferred stock, creating a dilution overhang that distinguishes it from Coherent's cleaner non-dilutive deal. The company's OCS (optical circuit switch) backlog surged past **$400 million**. Rosenblatt's price target of **$900** is the highest on the Street. The X thesis on LITE centers on its leap from "component price differences" to "system premium" through vertical integration — it achieved this by acquiring Cloud Light for $750 million in 2023 and now sits in the "lead pack" of transceiver suppliers for the 1.6T transition.
41	
42	**IQE plc (IQE)** sits at the base of the supply chain as the world's leading outsourced manufacturer of compound semiconductor epiwafers. The stock surged **88% in five trading days** in late February 2026 on takeover speculation, with a strategic review ongoing under the UK Takeover Code since September 2025 — Lazard is advising on potential offers for the company as a whole. IQE's FY2025 revenue was approximately **£97 million**, with photonics demand from AI/data center markets providing the brightest spot in an otherwise challenged business. The company launched a **6-inch InP foundry platform** to serve future silicon photonics demand and secured multiple Tier 1 design wins for laser and detector products enabling next-gen AI infrastructure. At a market cap of only ~£156-186 million (~$195-230 million), IQE represents a high-risk, high-reward play on the InP substrate bottleneck that constrains the entire photonics supply chain. The X discussion around IQE focuses on its potential as an acquisition target — its InP epiwafer capabilities become strategically critical as demand outstrips supply.
43	
44	### The dollar math at hyperscale
45	
46	SemiAnalysis provides the most granular independent cost analysis. For NVIDIA's Q3400/Q3450 class switch, **72 pluggable 1.6T transceivers cost approximately $72,000**, while CPO optical engines plus external laser sources have a raw cost of **$35,000–$40,000** but reach **$80,000–$90,000** at the buyer level after vendor gross margins of approximately 60%. At the networking level, CPO delivers **31% lower total networking cost** versus DSP transceivers by eliminating transceiver overhead and reducing SerDes complexity. At the total cluster level, however, server racks dominate spending, limiting CPO's impact to approximately **3% total cluster cost reduction**—or up to **7%** when CPO enables network flattening from three layers to two (which cuts transceiver costs by **86%** and total networking cost by **46%**).
47	
48	## Conclusion: the investment logic has shifted, but valuation discipline still matters
49	
50	The key risk that the X discussion underemphasizes is valuation. COHR at ~35x forward earnings, LITE after a 967% one-year run, and AAOI at 173% YTD gains all embed significant growth expectations. A deceleration in hyperscaler capex, a delay in 1.6T ramp timing, or faster-than-expected CPO cannibalization of pluggable transceivers (hurting AAOI's core business) could create sharp corrections in names trading at these multiples. The next major catalyst is **OFC 2026 (March 17-19)** and **NVIDIA GTC (March 16)**, where partnership confirmations and CPO product roadmaps will either reinforce or challenge the current pricing. For investors, the highest-conviction insight from this analysis is that **the laser and component layer — the "picks and shovels" of photonics — offers the most durable positioning** because these companies supply both pluggable and CPO architectures, insulating them from technology-path risk while capturing the full volume of the optical buildout.
51	
52	The core takeaway for your analysis: COHR is the most "reasonably" valued of the group on forward estimates given its scale and visibility, but even at ~36x forward P/E it's pricing in heavy execution on InP ramp and 1.6T transition. LITE is richer on every metric but has the highest revenue growth trajectory. AAOI, AXTI, and IQE are all pre-profit and priced purely on the optionality of supply chain positioning — classic momentum/narrative names where traditional valuation frameworks struggle to provide guardrails. The three unprofitable names carry the most downside risk if the CPO timeline slips or hyperscaler capex decelerates.
53	
54	Morningstar shows P/S (TTM) at 13.4x with no meaningful trailing P/E (the company is GAAP unprofitable). The forward P/E on Intellectia was listed at ~217x, though this seems to be using near-term estimates before the $1B+ revenue guide kicks in. TTM revenue is ~$456M with losses of -$156M, ROE of -40%, and ROIC of -8%. If management delivers on $1B+ CY2026 revenue guidance, forward P/S drops to ~7x with the EV potentially compressing further given positive operating income guidance (~$120M non-GAAP). But on current financials this is emphatically a "pay for growth" story — no trailing P/E or EV/EBIT is computable because EBIT is deeply negative. One analysis flagged forward P/E of ~48x, P/B above 4.9x, and P/S above 6.6x as reflecting growth expectations well ahead of current fundamentals.
55	
56	On modulators, which are the most competitively differentiated component, there are two dominant architectures. Mach-Zehnder Modulators (MZM) split light into two arms, apply voltage to change refractive index, then recombine. They're broadband and thermally stable but large (~1-4mm). Micro-Ring Modulators (MRM) are tiny resonant structures (~5-10μm radius) that are extremely compact but narrowband and thermally sensitive, requiring active wavelength locking circuits. TSMC Director Shih-Fen Huang highlighted at SEMICON Taiwan that MZMs excel in high-speed, high-power scenarios while MRMs provide compact, high-density advantages, making them core components for efficient CPO transmission. TSMC's successful demonstration of MRMs on its 3nm process at ISSCC 2026 is the breakthrough making high-density CPO viable.
57	
58	The third post (status/2023220154822447405) is "Lecture 3: The Devil is in the Details" — their technical deep dive into the specific components: waveguides, modulators (MRM vs MZM), photodetectors, couplers, and the critical external laser question. PhotonCap has explicitly stated that "Micro-Ring-Modulator-based CPO is the clear future," referencing the ISSCC 2026 papers. Key claims include that the critical bottleneck is not photonic circuit design but optical testing and characterization (dramatically slower than electrical testing), and that laser integration remains the single highest-risk element because co-packaging a laser with a hot ASIC creates thermal stress that rapidly degrades laser diodes. PhotonCap's broader framework emphasizes picks-and-shovels: TSEM for foundry, FORM for optical test/metrology, LITE for lasers, GLW for fiber, GFS for foundry, and POET for hybrid integration.
59	
60	The photonics trade has entered a new phase. The structural thesis is sound — AI infrastructure scaling physically requires the copper-to-optical transition, and the companies controlling laser capacity, silicon photonics integration, and InP manufacturing sit at the chokepoint. **LITE and COHR are now strategic NVIDIA partners**, not just vendors, creating a visibility and pricing power dynamic that supports multi-year earnings growth. AAOI offers the highest-beta exposure with its guided revenue doubling and Microsoft/hyperscaler dependency. IQE is a speculative play on the substrate bottleneck and potential acquisition premium.
61	
62	iST observes three recurring barriers that consistently slow mass-production readiness. Optical characterization requires full wavelength sweeps and repeated alignment for every die — unlike electrical probing, there is no instant plug-and-test. Conventional wafer acceptance test tools can indicate degradation but cannot localize the loss mechanism or quantify its spatial origin. These constraints make optical test dramatically slower, hindering design turnaround and delaying production ramp.
63	
64	---
65

3	tags: [research, semiconductors]
4	status: active

7	---
8	
9	# Chinese Silicon Photonics Threat
10	
11	## Original Query
12	> Assess the market share and competitive threat in the Copackged Optics / Silicon Photonics space from the Chinese supply chain. How does this translate to competitive risk for the US/Western dominated supply chain’s listed stocks like Lumentum / Coherent / AAOI etc.
13	> Thanks good work
14	
15	---
16	
17	## How each Western stock faces the competitive threat
18	
19	**Coherent Corp (COHR)** is the most directly exposed. As the #2 global transceiver vendor, Coherent competes head-to-head with Innolight and Eoptolink for hyperscaler volume. FY2025 revenue hit a record **$5.81B** (+23% YoY) with networking growth of 45–56%, and datacom revenue surged 79% YoY in Q2 — but this growth is market-driven, not share-driven. Coherent holds roughly 20% of Nvidia's optical wallet. Its estimated production costs run **10–20% higher than Innolight's**, creating structural margin pressure as the market matures. The defensive strategy centers on vertical integration (in-house InP lasers, VCSELs, and silicon photonics PICs) and a pivot toward optical circuit switches for AI datacenters. Coherent's FY2026 Q2 data center revenue grew 36% YoY. Analyst consensus is Strong Buy with a median price target of ~$240 and market cap of ~$48.5B, but the bull case depends on component leadership rather than module volume growth.
20	
21	**Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI)** is the most vulnerable. With ~$456M in 2025 revenue and ongoing net losses, AAOI is dwarfed by both Chinese and Western competitors. Its US-based manufacturing (Sugar Land, TX) carries **3x higher labor costs** than Chinese/Southeast Asian alternatives. Customer concentration is extreme — 95.8% of revenue comes from its top 10 customers, with Microsoft as the anchor. AAOI received a multi-million-dollar 800G OSFP order from a North American hyperscaler in December 2025, but short-seller Culper Research has questioned the company's 800G manufacturing claims. At ~$5B market cap with a beta of 7.18, AAOI trades as a speculative option on AI infrastructure spending rather than a structural competitive advantage. Analyst consensus is Buy with an average target of ~$49, but the company's survival in a market dominated by Innolight-class competitors is uncertain.
22	
23	**Marvell (MRVL)** benefits from its PAM4 DSP near-duopoly but faces long-term disruption. Q3 FY2026 revenue hit a record **$2.1B** (+37% YoY) with data center at $1.5B. The $3.25B acquisition of Celestial AI (completed February 2026) positions Marvell in photonic fabric interconnects for scale-up connectivity, expected to contribute **$500M annually by late 2028**. However, the LPO trend, Nvidia's in-house DSP development, and eventual Chinese DSP alternatives all threaten Marvell's core optical DSP franchise. Analyst consensus is Strong Buy with an average target of ~$116.
24	
25	**Cisco** is playing a longer game through its Acacia integration, which yielded the **Kibo 3nm 1.6T PAM4 DSP** — entering direct competition with Marvell and Broadcom in client optics. Silicon One G300 (102.4 Tbps) was announced in February 2026 for AI clusters, with AI infrastructure orders from hyperscalers reaching $2.1B in Q2 FY2026. Cisco is pursuing both CPO and LPO but maintaining a measured timeline, with CEO Robbins stating CPO will happen but "is not imminent."
26	
27	**Lumentum (LITE)** has perhaps the most compelling strategic pivot. FY2026 Q2 EPS of **$1.67 beat consensus by 36%**, and the company guided to ~$790M Q3 revenue. Stock appreciated ~287% in 2025. Lumentum is deliberately retreating from direct transceiver competition and repositioning as a **component supplier** — EML chips, pump lasers, CW lasers for CPO, and silicon photonics modules through its Cloud Light subsidiary (acquired for ~$750M). The company closed two photonic factories in China and is shifting production to Thailand and a new US indium phosphide wafer fab for CPO components. Management targets "significant revenue ramp in CPO by 2026." Lumentum is named as a CPO ecosystem partner for both Nvidia and Broadcom. Average analyst price target is ~$505 (13 analysts, consensus Buy), with Rosenblatt modeling $14–19 FY2027 EPS in upside scenarios. The risk is that CPO timelines slip and Chinese competitors close the laser technology gap.
28	
29	**Broadcom** holds the strongest structural position through vertical integration of switch ASICs (Tomahawk/Jericho), silicon photonics, and CPO. Its Bailly CPO platform is the **only CPO solution validated in production** at a hyperscaler (Meta). Broadcom's third-generation 200G/lane CPO (Davisson) was previewed in May 2025, and a 102.4 Tbps CPO switch is expected in H2 2026. If CPO captures a significant share of the optical interconnect market (projected >$20B by 2036), Broadcom could effectively disintermediate both Chinese and Western transceiver module vendors. This makes Broadcom the primary beneficiary of the CPO transition and the greatest long-term threat to the pluggable transceiver market.
30	
31	# China's optical dominance threatens Western photonics incumbents
32	
33	**Chinese manufacturers now control more than half the global high-speed optical transceiver market, dominating the 800G generation and positioning to capture 50–60% of 1.6T shipments — yet they remain critically dependent on Western DSP chips and laser technology, and trail by 3–5 years in co-packaged optics.** This asymmetry defines the competitive threat: Chinese module assemblers are winning the volume battle today, while Western firms retreat up the value chain into components, CPO integration, and silicon IP where margins and defensibility are higher. The AI infrastructure supercycle, projected to push the optical transceiver market past **$23 billion in 2025** (LightCounting), is temporarily masking this structural market share shift — every major Western optical stock posted record revenues in 2025 even as their unit share eroded. But as growth normalizes toward 2027–2028, the pricing pressure from Chinese competitors operating with **20–25% lower costs** will become the dominant narrative.
34	
35	## The 1.6T generation reinforces Chinese leadership, but CPO changes the game
36	
37	Chinese firms are pursuing CPO but lag significantly. Huagong Zhengyuan demonstrated a **3.2T DWDM CPO optical engine** at CIOE 2025, and Innolight has tested 3.2T optical engines — but no Chinese company has CPO in commercial production. The critical bottleneck is advanced packaging: TSMC's COUPE platform controls the integration of photonic chips with switching ASICs, and Chinese foundries have nothing comparable. The CPO market, valued at just ~$120M in 2025, is projected to reach **$20B+ by 2036** (IDTechEx), representing a potential paradigm shift that could redirect value from Chinese module assemblers to Western chip-and-packaging leaders.
38	
39	Innolight was the first vendor to complete **1.6T transceiver testing with Nvidia** and began phased shipments in Q2 2025. The company is projected to capture **50–60% of the 1.6T module market** in 2025, with volume inflection expected in 2026 when shipments could reach 3–5 million units. Eoptolink launched a fully retimed 1.6T OSFP with 3nm DSP at OFC 2025 and plans mass production of its VCSEL-based 1.6T variant by Q4 2025. Accelink's 1.6T monthly capacity already reaches **500,000 units**.
40	
41	## Conclusion: a bifurcating market with different winners at each layer
42	
43	The optical interconnect market is splitting into two distinct competitive arenas with different dynamics. In **pluggable transceivers** (800G through 1.6T), Chinese dominance is structural and accelerating — Innolight and Eoptolink have superior scale economics, faster time-to-market, and consistent profitability that Western module competitors cannot match. This arena will see persistent pricing pressure, with Chinese vendors pricing 20–25% below Western alternatives while maintaining higher margins. AAOI and Coherent's module business face the most direct competitive threat here.
44	
45	In **co-packaged optics and silicon photonic integration**, Western companies hold a 3–5 year lead that could prove durable. Broadcom, Nvidia, and Marvell are integrating optics with switch silicon and AI accelerators at a level of system complexity that Chinese module assemblers cannot replicate — particularly given TSMC's central role in advanced packaging. The CPO market is projected to grow from ~$120M today to over $20B within a decade. If CPO captures even 30–40% of high-speed optical interconnect spending by 2030, it would fundamentally restructure who captures value in the optical supply chain.
46	
47	The most important insight is that **the AI supercycle is temporarily obscuring a structural market share shift**. With the datacom optical market growing 60%+ annually, every company is posting record revenues regardless of competitive position. When growth normalizes toward 15–20% annually in 2027–2028, the pricing pressure from Chinese competitors will become the dominant investment narrative for Western optical stocks. The Western companies best positioned are those pivoting toward component-level and system-level integration — Lumentum in lasers and CPO components, Broadcom in full-stack CPO, and Marvell in DSP and photonic fabric — rather than those competing directly for pluggable transceiver volume against Innolight's manufacturing machine.
48	
49	**Biggest open question:** Whether CPO timelines slip (again). Broadcom and Nvidia are committed, but actual hyperscaler deployment at scale beyond Meta's initial rollout remains to be seen. If CPO gets pushed out to 2028+, the pluggable transceiver market (and Chinese dominance of it) persists longer — which is actually better for Innolight/Eoptolink and worse for the CPO-centric Western bull thesis.
50	
51	**Near-term catalysts to watch:** Broadcom's Bailly CPO ramp at Meta through 2026, Innolight's 1.6T volume inflection (likely H2 2026), and whether the 200G EML laser shortage eases — that bottleneck is currently the single biggest constraint on the entire 1.6T transition.
52	
53	**The "hidden" risk for bulls on Western optical names:** If you're long COHR or LITE primarily on the AI transceiver volume story, it's worth stress-testing what happens when growth decelerates to ~15–20% in 2027–28 and Innolight is still pricing 20%+ below. The stocks that hold up best will be the ones that have successfully pivoted to CPO components and vertical integration by then.
54	
55	## Trade policy provides limited protection as Chinese firms adapt
56	
57	Export controls are more targeted but also limited in their optical-specific impact. The 42 PRC entities added to the Entity List in March 2025 and 23 more in September 2025 primarily target advanced logic chips and GPUs, not optical components directly. The indirect vulnerability for Chinese transceiver makers lies in their dependence on Western DSPs manufactured at TSMC on 3nm/5nm nodes — but restricting DSP sales to Chinese optical vendors would immediately disrupt supply to Nvidia and all major hyperscalers, making such action self-defeating. The Trump-Xi Busan meeting in late 2025 produced a mutual tariff reduction, and the new USTR Section 301 investigation specifically targeting semiconductors set an initial tariff of 0%, with increases not scheduled until June 2027.
58	
59	The tariff regime on Chinese optical components is theoretically severe — **25% Section 301 tariffs** plus 20% IEEPA fentanyl duties plus regular MFN duties could bring total effective tariffs to 55–65%+ on Chinese-origin transceivers. But the practical impact has been minimal. Chinese manufacturers have systematically shifted final assembly to Thailand, Vietnam, and Malaysia under a **"China design + Southeast Asia manufacturing"** model, with combined monthly capacity exceeding **1.5 million units**. LightCounting's July 2025 assessment was unambiguous: "The trade war has had no significant impact on sales of optical transceivers to US-based Cloud companies."
60	
61	## Critical dependencies give Western firms structural leverage
62	
63	- **High-speed lasers:** EML and InP laser technology for 200G-per-lane applications remains dominated by Coherent and Lumentum. A severe shortage of **200G EML chips** currently constrains the entire 1.6T ramp, with Nvidia reportedly assisting suppliers to accelerate production. Source Photonics has made progress with in-house EML and monolithic InP PICs, but Coherent and Lumentum retain the technology lead. China's JFS Laboratory in Wuhan achieved the country's first integrated laser-on-silicon demonstration, but this is R&D-stage, not production-ready.
64	
65	The **LPO (Linear Pluggable Optics)** trend partially mitigates DSP dependency by eliminating the DSP chip entirely, using only analog driver/TIA chips. Eoptolink has aggressively commercialized LPO for specific hyperscale customers. But DSP-based retimed optics remain the interoperability standard, and Nvidia is developing its own 1.6T DSP (via its Mellanox team) that could eventually cover >50% of its own volume — further squeezing Chinese module makers' wallet share at the system vendor level.
66	
67	- **PAM4 DSP chips:** Marvell holds **80%+ share** at 800G speeds, with Broadcom as the primary alternative. These chips represent ~30% of transceiver BOM cost, and Chinese vendors are entirely dependent on them. Marvell's Ara (3nm, 1.6T) won the industry's Leading EDGE Award in 2025; no Chinese alternative operates at competitive performance. Over a dozen Chinese DSP startups exist (Aluksen, EOChip, Hengxin Semitech), but **none have achieved meaningful market penetration** at 800G/1.6T speeds. LightCounting estimates Chinese DSP development trails by **2–3 years**.
68	
69	- **Advanced packaging for CPO:** TSMC's COUPE platform is the de facto standard for next-generation CPO, adopted by both Nvidia and Broadcom. Chinese packaging capability has no equivalent. However, silicon photonics fabrication itself does **not require EUV lithography**, giving China a potential long-term pathway using domestic DUV equipment — a strategically significant fact as export controls tighten.
70	
71	---
72
